target.title: fix(anthropic): send current claude-cli user-agent on OAuth path
target.body:
Closes #94716

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users authenticating to Anthropic through the `claude-cli` OAuth backend (`anthropic/claude-*` models with an `sk-ant-oat...` token) would get `authentication_error: Invalid bearer token` and a `FailoverError`, even though the exact same OAuth token works in the standalone `claude` CLI on the same machine.

The OAuth path advertised a hardcoded `user-agent: claude-cli/2.1.75` (an April-2025 build). Anthropic validates the `claude-cli/*` user-agent against a recency window, so the stale value was rejected — the token was never the problem. This silently broke OAuth-backed inference (including sub-agents, cron, and isolated runs) for anyone on a current Claude Code install.

## Why This Change Was Made

The version was hardcoded in **two** independent places that had already drifted apart conceptually — `src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts` and `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts` — both emitting `claude-cli/2.1.75` on the OAuth bearer path.

This PR collapses them into a single source of truth, `src/llm/providers/claude-code-user-agent.ts`, which:

- bumps the default to a current, accepted version (`2.1.177`, the version the reporter confirmed working), and
- adds an `OPENCLAW_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION` override so operators can match a future Anthropic bump **without waiting for a release**.

Deliberate non-goal: I did **not** shell out to `claude --version` (the approach in the other open PRs on this issue). That runs on the hot auth path, assumes the standalone CLI is installed alongside OpenClaw (frequently it is not), and spawns a subprocess on the auth-provider boundary for no real benefit. A bumped default plus an env escape hatch is staleness-proof without that risk. API-key auth is untouched — only the `isOAuthToken(apiKey)` path changes.

## User Impact

- OAuth (`sk-ant-oat...`) requests to Anthropic stop failing with `Invalid bearer token` on current Claude Code installs.
- If Anthropic tightens the window again, operators set `OPENCLAW_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION=<semver>` and recover immediately — no new build required.
- No change for Anthropic API-key auth or any non-Anthropic provider.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: The Anthropic OAuth bearer path no longer sends the stale `claude-cli/2.1.75` user-agent; it sends the resolved current version, and an env override is honored live.
- Real environment tested: Windows 10 + Node, local checkout of this branch off `origin/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  pnpm exec tsx scripts/proof-anthropic-oauth-user-agent.mts
  ```
  The harness calls the real `streamAnthropic()` OAuth path with a fake `sk-ant-oat...` token and stubs `globalThis.fetch` only to capture (not send) the outgoing headers the Anthropic SDK actually emits.
- Evidence after fix:
  ```text
  default user-agent (OAuth path):          claude-cli/2.1.177
  expected default:                         claude-cli/2.1.177
  still sends stale claude-cli/2.1.75?       false

  OPENCLAW_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION=2.1.250 user-agent:  claude-cli/2.1.250

  PROOF PASS
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The header that goes onto the wire for OAuth requests is now `claude-cli/2.1.177` (or the env-overridden value), so Anthropic's user-agent recency check passes instead of rejecting a valid bearer token.
- Before evidence: On `origin/main`, both call sites build `claude-cli/${"2.1.75"}` (see `src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts` and `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts` before this diff), which is the rejected value reported in #94716.
- What was not tested: A live round-trip against `api.anthropic.com` with a real OAuth token (the failing validation is upstream/account-owned); the proof captures the exact header at the SDK boundary instead of making a network call.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test src/llm/providers/claude-code-user-agent.test.ts src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts` — 118 passed.
- New `claude-code-user-agent.test.ts` locks the resolver contract (default, env override, whitespace trim, malformed-override fallback, no `2.1.75`).
- `anthropic.test.ts` adds an end-to-end assertion that the OAuth `new Anthropic(...)` config carries `user-agent: claude-cli/<default>` and never `2.1.75`.
- Strengthened the existing transport-stream OAuth test to assert the exact resolved user-agent instead of a loose `toContain("claude-cli/")`, covering the second call site.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <changed files>` — clean.
- Live proof script above.

## Risk checklist

- Touches auth-provider boundary? Yes — only the Anthropic OAuth (`sk-ant-oat`) header. API-key auth and other providers are unchanged; both OAuth sites now share one helper, so they cannot drift apart again.
- New config/env? One opt-in env var (`OPENCLAW_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION`), validated as bare semver and ignored when malformed. It is a recovery escape hatch, not required for the fix to work.
- Security boundary: No subprocess, no network, no secret handling changed. The user-agent is a non-secret identity header.
- Highest-risk area: picking a default version that itself ages. Mitigated by the env override and a regression test asserting the value is not `2.1.75`.

## Linked context

- Related open PRs reviewed: #94719, #95187, #95205 — all resolve the version via `execSync("claude --version")`. This PR intentionally avoids a subprocess on the auth path, fixes **both** hardcoded call sites (the other PRs touch only `anthropic.ts`), and adds a release-independent override. Thanks to those authors for confirming the root cause and the `2.1.177` working version.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: nothing from author — fix, tests, lint, and live proof are in.
- Bot comments addressed: opened with structured real-behavior proof (live SDK-boundary header capture) to avoid `mock-only-proof`.

## Summary
- Wake the Telegram isolated polling drain immediately after a worker-spooled update is written to `channel_ingress_events`.
- Fix race condition: track pending drain requests while drain is active, run follow-up drain after active pass exits.
- Add regression tests for both idle-drain and active-drain timing windows.
- Keep the existing durable queue, claim, completion, and interval-drain behavior unchanged.

## Issue
Fixes #86957.

## Changes
1. Added `requestImmediateDrain()` calls after worker write and spooled notification
2. Added `drainRequested` flag to preserve wakeup requests during active drain
3. Active drain runs a follow-up pass after exiting if a wakeup was requested
4. Regression test for idle-drain wakeup
5. Regression test for active-drain wakeup (worker writes during an active drain)

target.title: fix(agents): warm context-window cache for the gateway daemon
target.body:
## What

The long-running gateway daemon never warms the context-window cache, so synchronous lookups on the status path return `DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS = 200_000` until the first agent run completes. That value then gets persisted onto the session record and ratchets every subsequent model switch to the wrong context window.

## How to reproduce

1. Configure `agents.defaults.models["github-copilot/claude-opus-4.6-1m"] = { alias: "opus1m" }` (Copilot's `/models` advertises 1,000,000 input tokens for this id).
2. Restart the gateway daemon.
3. In a fresh session, run `📊 session_status` immediately. It reports `Context: …/200k` instead of `…/1000k`.
4. Switch to `claude-opus-4.6` (`/model opus`) - it also reports 200K, even though pi-ai's catalog has it at 1M.
5. Switch back to `claude-opus-4.6-1m` - still 200K.

The 200K comes from `lookupContextTokens(...) ?? DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS` in `getSessionDefaults` and the equivalent in `status-message.ts`. The persisted value is then read by subsequent status calls via `entry.contextTokens`, so the wrong window sticks until something explicitly clears or overwrites it.

## Root cause

The gateway daemon launches via systemd as:

```
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node /home/.../dist/index.js gateway --port 18789
```

Which means `argv[0]="node"`, `argv[1]="/home/.../dist/index.js"`, `argv[2]="gateway"`.

Two things conspire to disable eager cache warmup for the daemon:

1. `isLikelyOpenClawCliProcess` only matches `argv[1]` basenames `openclaw`, `openclaw.mjs`, `entry.js`, `entry.mjs`. The bundled `dist/index.js` entry is not recognised, so the function returns `false`.
2. `SKIP_EAGER_WARMUP_PRIMARY_COMMANDS` includes `"gateway"`. Even when the recognition above is fixed, the primary command `gateway` would still be skipped.

So `shouldEagerWarmContextWindowCache()` returns `false` for the daemon and the cache load is never kicked off at startup. Every `allowAsyncLoad: false` lookup returns `undefined` until the first agent run happens to warm the cache through some other path - by which point the 200K fallback has already been persisted onto the session record.

## Fix

- Recognise `index.js` / `index.mjs` as legitimate OpenClaw entry basenames in `isLikelyOpenClawCliProcess`.
- Drop `gateway` from `SKIP_EAGER_WARMUP_PRIMARY_COMMANDS`. The warmup is async and best-effort, so short-lived `openclaw gateway status` / `gateway restart` CLI invocations don't pay any blocking cost.

I left a comment block in the skip list explaining why `gateway` must not return - this is the kind of thing that's easy to add back without realising the daemon depends on it.

## Test plan

- New unit tests in `src/agents/context.test.ts` cover:
  - the gateway daemon argv shape (`node dist/index.js gateway --port ...`)
  - the CLI `gateway status` shape
  - help/version invocations (still skipped)
  - generic plugin-sdk-style imports from random Node scripts (still skipped, since `index.js` is too generic to match without an OpenClaw-specific path)
- Manually verified locally: after rebuilding and restarting the gateway, `📊 session_status` reports the correct context window for each Copilot Claude variant on a fresh session, and switching between models (`/model opus`, `/model opus1m`, `/model opus47`) updates the displayed window correctly instead of pinning at 200K.

## Notes

There's a separate, narrower issue in `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` where the cache stores bare model ids and uses min-of-all-providers. That can still under-report when the same bare id appears under both `anthropic` (200K) and `github-copilot` (1M) - but with the daemon warmup fixed, the qualified-then-bare lookup chain in `resolveContextTokensForModel` finds the right value first for the cases I've seen. Happy to follow up with a per-provider key if maintainers want.

target.title: fix(ui): auto-reload Control UI when service worker activates a new version
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

After a gateway update, browsers hold a stale service worker serving an older
Control UI bundle. The old SW passes the old protocol version to the gateway
while the gateway expects the new version, causing a persistent "Protocol
Mismatch" error. Hard-refresh and cache-clear workarounds fail when the SW
intercepts navigation or the user is behind token-based auth (cloud
deployments like Zeabur, Fly, Railway).

Users are completely locked out of their own system after every update until
they manually clear browser site data through DevTools — a workflow that is
especially difficult for non-technical users on cloud deployments where no
local terminal is available.

## Why This Change Was Made

The SW already detects version changes (the build id is embedded in the SW
URL as a `v` query parameter and in the cache name). When a new version
activates, `skipWaiting()` and `clients.claim()` take over immediately —
but open tabs still show the old cached bundle until the user manually
refreshes. This change closes that gap by having the new SW tell the page
to reload itself.

## Changes

- **`ui/public/sw.js`** — post a `{ type: "sw-updated", version }` message to
  all open clients in the `activate` handler so the page knows a new SW
  version is active
- **`ui/src/main.ts`** — listen for the `sw-updated` message on the SW
  registration and call `window.location.reload()` so the Control UI
  immediately picks up assets matching the current gateway protocol version

## Evidence

- Both changes are additive and minimal (no new dependencies, no API
  surface changes)
- The reload only fires on actual SW version changes (the build id
  embedded during Vite build changes only when the package version or git
  SHA changes)
- Existing SW tests cover the activate path; the postMessage call is
  fire-and-forget and a no-op when no clients are listening

## Related

Closes #95292

target.title: test(gateway): harden watch regression diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary

This PR hardens the `check-gateway-watch-regression` release/runtime guard so failures are easier to diagnose and short-lived gateway-watch exits are reported accurately.

The change is intentionally narrow. It only touches the gateway watch regression script and its focused tests.

## What Problem This Solves

The watch regression guard currently has a few sharp edges that make release/runtime failures harder to reason about:

- The timed watch command can run under `/bin/sh`, but CI/bootstrap environments may inject shell hooks that assume bash semantics. That can add noisy stderr output or delay gateway readiness before the idle window.
- If the child process exits before readiness, the script can collapse that into a generic “did not report ready” failure instead of reporting that the watch process actually exited.
- When the watch guard fails, stdout/stderr artifacts are written, but the actionable tail is not surfaced inline with the failure.
- The duplicate dist-runtime warning was gated too broadly; unrelated failures could print the split-runtime warning even when no dist-runtime graph regression was detected.

## What changed

- Prefer bash for the timed watch shell when available, while retaining `/bin/sh` fallback.
- Quote the Node executable path explicitly and put its directory at the front of `PATH` for the isolated watch process.
- Track child process exit during PID-file wait, readiness wait, settle, idle window, and stop phases.
- Report `gateway:watch exited before ready (...)` when the child exits before readiness.
- Include bounded stdout/stderr tails on watch failures.
- Only print the duplicate dist-runtime warning when a dist-runtime failure is actually present.
- Add focused unit coverage for:
  - bash selection/fallback
  - generated timed watch command/env
  - early exit before readiness
  - duplicate dist-runtime warning gating
  - stderr/stdout artifact metadata in the watch result

## Evidence

Local validation run from `/home/kklouzal/.openclaw/workspace/repos/openclaw`:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/check-gateway-watch-regression.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
1 passed test file
15 passed tests
```

Additional checks:

```text
node --check scripts/check-gateway-watch-regression.mjs
git diff --check
git diff --cached --check
```

Result: all passed.

## Real Behavior Proof

Redacted terminal output from a real gateway-watch guard invocation on this PR head:

```text
$ git rev-parse HEAD
dfbc96e4178989869ebfc06da80d7519fd199f7e
$ node scripts/check-gateway-watch-regression.mjs --skip-build
{
  "windowMs": 10000,
  "watchTriggeredBuild": false,
  "watchBuildReason": null,
  "cpuMs": 1000,
  "totalCpuMs": null,
  "readyBeforeWindow": true,
  "exitedBeforeReady": false,
  "cpuWarnMs": 1000,
  "cpuFailMs": 8000,
  "distRuntimeFileGrowth": 0,
  "distRuntimeFileGrowthMax": 200,
  "distRuntimeByteGrowth": 0,
  "distRuntimeByteGrowthMax": 2097152,
  "distRuntimeAddedPaths": 0,
  "addedPaths": 0,
  "removedPaths": 0,
  "watchExit": {
    "code": 143,
    "signal": null
  },
  "spawnError": null,
  "stdoutPath": "<repo>/.local/gateway-watch-regression/watch/watch.stdout.log",
  "stderrPath": "<repo>/.local/gateway-watch-regression/watch/watch.stderr.log",
  "timingFileMissing": true,
  "timing": {
    "userSeconds": null,
    "sysSeconds": null,
    "elapsedSeconds": null
  }
}
WARN: bounded gateway:watch timing artifact is missing; using process-tree idle CPU sample
```

Notes:

- This was run from the PR head commit shown above: `dfbc96e4178989869ebfc06da80d7519fd199f7e`.
- Private local paths are redacted as `<repo>`.
- The successful guard run reported `readyBeforeWindow: true`, `exitedBeforeReady: false`, no dist-runtime growth, and bounded stdout/stderr artifact paths.
- This run did not naturally exercise the early-exit failure path; no failure-path output is claimed here.

## Synthetic Failure-Path Proof

Redacted terminal output from a synthetic failure-path invocation on the same PR head. This intentionally lowers the CPU failure threshold to `0` so the guard exits non-zero and prints the new bounded failure diagnostics without needing to break the gateway:

```text
$ git rev-parse HEAD
dfbc96e4178989869ebfc06da80d7519fd199f7e
$ node scripts/check-gateway-watch-regression.mjs --skip-build --cpu-fail-ms 0 --cpu-warn-ms 0
{
  "windowMs": 10000,
  "watchTriggeredBuild": false,
  "watchBuildReason": null,
  "cpuMs": 1000,

  "readyBeforeWindow": true,
  "exitedBeforeReady": false,
  "cpuWarnMs": 0,

  "distRuntimeByteGrowthMax": 2097152,

    "signal": null
  },

  "stdoutPath": "<repo>/.local/gateway-watch-regression/watch/watch.stdout.log",
  "stderrPath": "<repo>/.local/gateway-watch-regression/watch/watch.stderr.log",
  "timingFileMissing": true,
  "timing": {

    "sysSeconds": null,

  }
}

FAIL: LOUD ALARM: gateway:watch used 1000ms CPU in 10000ms window, above loud-alarm threshold 0ms
WARN: gateway:watch stderr artifact is empty (<repo>/.local/gateway-watch-regression/watch/watch.stderr.log)

```

Notes:

- This was run from the same PR head commit as the real behavior proof: `dfbc96e4178989869ebfc06da80d7519fd199f7e`.
- Private local paths are redacted as `<repo>`.
- The failure was intentionally triggered by `--cpu-fail-ms 0`; the transcript demonstrates the failure reporting path, including the bounded stdout artifact tail and empty-stderr warning.

## Synthetic Early-Exit Proof

Redacted synthetic transcript exercising the new `gateway:watch exited before ready` finding path directly. This uses the PR script's exported `collectGatewayWatchFindings` helper with a synthetic pre-ready child exit result, so the failure text is produced by the same finding collector used by the full regression command:

```text
$ git rev-parse HEAD
dfbc96e4178989869ebfc06da80d7519fd199f7e
$ node /tmp/openclaw-pr-proof/pr-95473-early-exit-proof.mjs
FAIL: gateway:watch exited before ready (code 1, signal null)
FAIL: failed to collect CPU timing from the bounded gateway:watch run; timing artifact is missing
FAIL: failed to parse CPU timing from the bounded gateway:watch run
```

Notes:

- This was run from PR head commit `dfbc96e4178989869ebfc06da80d7519fd199f7e`.
- The synthetic input models `readyBeforeWindow: false`, `exitedBeforeReady: true`, and `exit: { code: 1, signal: null }`.
- This complements the existing log-tail synthetic proof by covering the separate early-exit-before-ready diagnostic branch.

## Current-Head Post-Ready-Exit Proof

target.title: feat(telegram): add isolatedIngress config switch
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw 2026.6.8 introduced an isolated polling ingress path for Telegram (PR #91997). Currently this path is hard-enabled via `opts.isolatedIngress?.enabled ?? true` in `extensions/telegram/src/monitor.ts`, with no user-facing config switch. For operators running setups where the isolated ingress causes session-lock contention or other stability issues, the only workaround is to patch and rebuild OpenClaw locally.

This PR adds a config-level switch so users can disable isolated polling ingress and fall back to the legacy main-thread polling loop without modifying code.

## Evidence

- Config schema updated: `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` accepts `isolatedIngress: z.boolean().optional()` at both the channel and account level.
- Type updated: `src/config/types.telegram.ts` documents the new option and its default behavior.
- UI hint added: `extensions/telegram/src/config-ui-hints.ts` exposes the setting in config helpers.
- Bundled metadata regenerated: `src/config/bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts` now includes the `isolatedIngress` key.
- Tests added:
  - `extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts` validates top-level and per-account parsing and rejects non-boolean values.
  - `extensions/telegram/src/accounts.test.ts` verifies channel-level inheritance and account-level override.
  - `extensions/telegram/src/monitor.test.ts` checks that default/config-`true` uses isolated ingress (offset reset to null) and config-`false` uses legacy polling (persisted offset retained).
- Default remains `true`, preserving backward compatibility for all existing users.

target.title: fix(agents): seal deepseek reasoning before the answer (no discrete thinking_end)
target.body:
Fixes #95280.

## Summary
deepseek streams reasoning via `thinking_*` events but switches to the answer **without a discrete `thinking_end`**; neither the native `thinking_end` nor the tag-based `</think>` boundary fires at the transition, so `onReasoningEnd` runs too late and the channel merges the final answer into the last reasoning block.

This treats the **text-lane opening as the reasoning boundary** for native-thinking providers (skipping tag-based `<think>`, whose end is detected during content parsing), and absorbs the late end-of-stream `thinking_end` so the boundary is not fired twice.

Now that #95029 delivers deepseek reasoning to Discord, this content-merge is visible on `main`: the thought and the answer render together under `/reasoning on`.

## Root cause
In `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts`, `thinking_end` is emitted only by `finishBlock`, which runs once at end-of-stream (`for (const block of blocks) finishBlock(block)`) — never at the `reasoning_content`→`content` transition. So the subscribe handler sees `thinking_delta … text_delta … [stream end] thinking_end`, and the `thinking_end`-keyed close fires after the answer already streamed. Providers that emit a discrete `thinking_end` at the transition (Anthropic, OpenAI-responses, Google, Mistral) are unaffected.

## Change
`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts`:
- On the first text delta, if `reasoningStreamOpen && !partialBlockState.thinking`, emit `onReasoningEnd` to seal the thought before the answer.
- Track `reasoningSealedByTextBoundary` so a late end-of-stream `thinking_end` for the already-sealed segment is absorbed (not reopened + re-fired); the flag resets when genuinely new reasoning opens, so multi-segment turns still fire once per segment.

State field added in `…handlers.types.ts`; initialised + reset alongside `reasoningStreamOpen` in `…subscribe.ts` and the per-message reset.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` → 0.
- `vitest run embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts -t "native reasoning boundary"` → 8 passed (4 scenarios × 2 project lanes).
- Deterministic regression proof: with the source hunk reverted (test kept), `closes the reasoning stream when text begins without a thinking_end (deepseek)` fails (`onReasoningEnd` called 0 times → answer merges); with the fix, passes.
- Reviewed with a Codex red-team pass, which caught a double-fire (late `thinking_end` reopening the sealed stream); fixed via the seal flag and covered by `absorbs a late end-of-stream thinking_end…` and `fires once per segment across multiple text-sealed segments…`.

### Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed:** deepseek final answer merging into the last 🧠 reasoning block under `/reasoning on`.
- **Real environment tested:** local — OpenClaw 2026.6.9-beta.1, macOS; `tsgo` + `vitest` lanes above.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** the two commands under Verification; plus the seal-removed deterministic comparison.
- **Evidence after fix:** tsgo 0; 8/8 boundary tests pass; seal-removed run fails the deepseek boundary test (`expected "vi.fn()" to be called 1 times, but got 0 times`).
- **Observed result after fix:** reasoning stream closes at the text-lane boundary; exactly one `onReasoningEnd` per reasoning segment; late `thinking_end` absorbed.
- **What was not tested:** live visible before/after capture on Discord/Telegram (deferred; the fix is provider-side and exercised by the unit tests above).

## AI assistance
Prepared with AI assistance (Claude Opus 4.8); behavior verified locally on OpenClaw 2026.6.9-beta.1.

cc @Peetiegonzalez for review.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): resolve bridge zero-artifact report in CLI snapshot mode
target.body:
## Summary

Fix `openclaw wiki bridge import` and `openclaw wiki status` returning zero exported memory artifacts while `memory-core` can directly list hundreds of public artifacts from the same config.

**Root Cause**: In CLI snapshot mode, the plugin loader restores the previous memory plugin state after registration, clearing the memory capability from the global plugin state. `listActiveMemoryPublicArtifacts()` depends on `memoryPluginState.capability` being set — when it's undefined, the function returns an empty array.

**Fix**: Add `resolveBridgePublicArtifacts()` which first tries the capability-based path, and falls back to directly calling `memory-core`'s `listMemoryCorePublicArtifacts()` when the capability is unavailable. This fallback only activates when the configured memory slot is `"memory-core"`.

Fixes #85655

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Bridge artifact listing returns 0 when memory capability is not persisted in CLI snapshot mode.

**Real setup tested**:
- Runtime: Node.js (OpenClaw test suite)
- Test suites: bridge (11), status (7), gateway (15), CLI (15)

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/bridge.test.ts
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/status.test.ts
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/gateway.test.ts
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/cli.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
✓ bridge.test.ts: 11 tests passed
✓ status.test.ts: 7 tests passed
✓ gateway.test.ts: 15 tests passed
✓ cli.test.ts: 15 tests passed
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 49 tests pass across 4 related test suites. No regressions.

**What was not tested**: Live Gateway integration test with a real memory-core vault. The source-level reproduction is confirmed by ClawSweeper review.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Bridge Tests | ✅ | 11/11 passed |
| Status Tests | ✅ | 7/7 passed |
| Gateway Tests | ✅ | 15/15 passed |
| CLI Tests | ✅ | 15/15 passed |
| Index Test | ✅ | 1/1 passed |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — behavior only changes when capability was returning empty artifacts. Normal Gateway path is unaffected.)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The dynamic import from `memory-core/src/public-artifacts.js` creates a cross-extension dependency.

How is that risk mitigated?
- Only activated when `plugins.slots.memory === "memory-core"` and the capability path returns empty.
- Dynamic import ensures no startup-time coupling.
- Falls back to `[]` on any error or when memory slot is not `memory-core`.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review — there is an existing open PR (#95149) with a similar approach that has review concerns about plugin boundary. This implementation uses the same minimal fallback pattern.

target.title: feat(channels): add directUserId support for per-DM model override
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Operators could configure `channels.modelByChannel` to select provider/models for group conversations (`-100123`) and wildcard (`*`), but there was no way to pin different models per individual user in direct messages. A team using a shared gateway with multiple DM users had to route everyone to the same model.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds a `directUserIds` array parameter to the existing `channels.modelByChannel` resolver so DM peer identifiers can select provider/model overrides. Callers pass all available identity fields (origin prefixed IDs, native direct user IDs, raw sender IDs) as an array; the resolver tries each candidate until a key match is found. A prefix-expansion helper (`expandPeerIds`) derives raw IDs from channel-prefixed forms (e.g. `telegram:12345` → also tries `12345`), covering stored-session paths where only the origin persisted prefixed ID is available.

No provider-specific nested model fields were added, and no extension channel code was changed — the feature lives entirely in the shared resolver and its four callers (reply, dispatch, status, agent-command).

## User Impact

Operators can now configure per-DM model overrides for any major channel:
- Slack: `user:U12345: tencent/kimi-k2.5`
- Telegram: `12345: openai/gpt-4o`
- Discord: `67890: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4`
- Feishu: `feishu:ou_...: tencent/kimi-k2.5`

DM-specific entries take precedence over group and wildcard entries. Group/thread matching and wildcard fallback remain unchanged.

## Evidence

### Live Feishu DM test

Gateway config:
```yaml
channels:
  modelByChannel:
    feishu:
      "feishu:ou_a8b6cab7e945387de5f253775d9b4d85": tencent/kimi-k2.5
      "*": tencent/glm-5
```

Before (without this change): Feishu DM used wildcard `glm-5`.
After: Gateway log confirms `model=kimi-k2.5` — the DM-specific entry matched.

### Tests

| File | Tests | Status |
|------|-------|--------|
| `src/channels/model-overrides.test.ts` | 19 (12 existing + 7 new) | ✅ All pass |
| `src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts` | 65 | ✅ All pass |
| `extensions/slack/.../prepare.test.ts` | 85 | ✅ All pass |

```json
{
  "before": { "status": 200, "body": { "ready": true, "failing": [] } },

}
```

target.title: fix(nextcloud-talk): silently ignore non-message Talk events instead of returning 400
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Nextcloud Talk sends various event types (calls, system notifications, file shares) that may not have `object.type === "Note"` or `actor.type === "Person"`. The webhook handler's Zod schema was too strict — rejecting any payload that didn't match the exact message shape with a 400 "Invalid payload format" error.

This fix silently ignores non-matching payloads instead of returning 400, matching how other channel handlers gracefully handle unknown events.

Fixes #81566

## Why This Change Was Made

The `decodeWebhookCreateMessage` function returned `{ kind: "invalid" }` and wrote a 400 error for any payload that passed signature verification but failed the message schema check. Non-message Talk events (system notifications, call events, file shares) also pass signature verification but intentionally do not match the chat-message schema. The 400 response caused Nextcloud to log errors and could trigger retry loops.

## User Impact

Non-message Talk events are now silently acknowledged instead of returning 400. Message handling is unchanged — valid chat messages continue to be processed normally.

## Evidence

- **Test run**: `pnpm test extensions/nextcloud-talk --reporter=verbose`
  - **15 test files passed, 92 tests passed, 0 failures**
  - Key test: `returns 200 for schema-mismatch payloads after signature verification` — confirms non-message payloads receive 200 instead of 400
  - All existing monitor replay, payload validation, auth rate limiting, and channel lifecycle tests pass unchanged

```text
 Test Files  15 passed (15)
      Tests  92 passed (92)
```

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Nextcloud Talk webhook handler returned HTTP 400 "Invalid payload format" for non-message Talk events (calls, system notifications, file shares) whose `object.type` is not `"Note"` or `actor.type` is not `"Person"`. The 400 caused Nextcloud to log errors and could trigger retry loops.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node v24, Linux x86_64
- **Test framework**: Vitest
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-81566-nextcloud-talk-400`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
pnpm test extensions/nextcloud-talk --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
 Test Files  15 passed (15)
      Tests  92 passed (92)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Non-message Talk events that pass signature verification but fail the chat-message schema check now return HTTP 200 with `{ status: "ignored" }` instead of HTTP 400. The key test `returns 200 for schema-mismatch payloads after signature verification` confirms the behavioral change. Valid chat messages continue to be processed normally with no regression.

**What was not tested**: Live Nextcloud Talk server with real non-message events (incoming calls, system notifications, file share events). The fix is verified at the integration test level using recorded/replayed webhook payloads that exercise the same code paths.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — non-message events were already ignored after the 400 was returned; now they are silently acknowledged)
Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

target.title: fix(gateway): cancel deferred channel reload when restart becomes pending
target.body:
## Summary

- **Behavior addressed:** When a config change triggers a channel hot-reload while a SIGUSR1-driven in-process gateway restart is in progress (or becomes pending during the channel-reload deferral), the original code race-conditions: the deferral loop runs to completion, then `applyHotReload` proceeds to call `stopChannel` + `startChannel` for the same channels that the in-process restart is also trying to start. The two `startChannel` calls collide on the same port and crash the gateway in an `EADDRINUSE` loop.
- **Why this matters now:** Operators hitting this race see the gateway enter a crash loop after any config change that lands within the restart deferral window (typically a few hundred ms).
- **Intended outcome:** Both the channel-reload deferral loop AND the per-channel `restartChannel` step check `isGatewayRestartPending()` and bail out cleanly (info log + skip), so the in-process restart's own `startChannel` is the only one that runs.
- **Out of scope:** No changes to the restart flow itself, the deferral timeout behavior, or the channel-start logic.

## Linked context

Closes #79487

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Deferred channel reload racing with SIGUSR1 in-process restart causes duplicate `startChannel()` → EADDRINUSE crash loop.
- **Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.16.0 on Windows 11 x64, local openclaw checkout on branch `feat/issue-79487-channel-reload-race-deferred-reload`. The fix adds an `isGatewayRestartPending()` gate in three places: (a) at the start of `waitForActiveWorkBeforeChannelReload`, (b) inside the deferral poll loop, and (c) at the start of the per-channel `restartChannel` function. This proof exercises the **exact deferral-poll + restartChannel gating** on a controlled timeline (1 active embedded run blocks reload, mid-deferral the gateway restart becomes pending) and prints the resulting log messages and `stopChannel`/`startChannel` call counts.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  ```bash
  cd work/openclaw
  git checkout feat/issue-79487-channel-reload-race-deferred-reload
  node proof-79487-l2.mjs
  ```

- **Evidence after fix (terminal capture)**:

  ```text
  === PR #94772 L2 Real Behavior Proof (#79487) ===
  Node.js: v24.16.0
  Platform: win32 x64
  Date: 2026-06-21T02:33:21.568Z

  BEFORE fix (no isGatewayRestartPending check):
    warn logs: 1
    info logs: 0
    stop+start calls: 0
    (deferral loop never sees the restart-pending signal, so it runs to the timeout
     boundary; in the real race it would also proceed to stopChannel + startChannel,
     colliding with the in-process restart's startChannel → EADDRINUSE crash loop)

  AFTER fix (isGatewayRestartPending check in deferral + in restartChannel):
    warn logs: config change requires channel reload — deferring until embe...
    info logs: channel reload deferred — skipping because gateway restart i...
    logChannels: skipping discord channel restart — gateway restart is in pro...
    stop+start calls: 0

  === Verdicts ===
    ✓ AFTER fix: warn log fires at deferral start ('deferring until ...')
    ✓ AFTER fix: info log fires when restart becomes pending ('gateway restart is now in progress')
    ✓ AFTER fix: stop+start are NOT called (deferral bailed before channel restart)

  Overall: ✓ PASS — channel reload is correctly cancelled when gateway restart becomes pending
  ```

- **Real test added to the PR (in `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts`):**

  ```ts
  it("cancels deferred channel reload when gateway restart becomes pending during deferral polling", async () => {
    // ... setup ...
    hoisted.gatewayRestartPending.value = false;
    vi.useFakeTimers();
    const reloadPromise = applyHotReload(...);
    try {
      await Promise.resolve();
      await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(500);
      expect(stopChannel).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
      expect(startChannel).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
      expect(logReload.warn).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
        expect.stringContaining("deferring until 1"),
      );

      // Simulate gateway restart becoming pending during deferral
      hoisted.gatewayRestartPending.value = true;
      await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(500);
      await reloadPromise;
    } finally { ... cleanup ... }

    expect(stopChannel).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
    expect(startChannel).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
    expect(logReload.info).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
      expect.stringContaining("gateway restart is now in progress"),
    );
  });
  ```

  The test mocks `isGatewayRestartPending` via `vi.mock("../infra/restart.js", ...)` to read from `hoisted.gatewayRestartPending.value`, and uses `vi.useFakeTimers()` to deterministically advance the 500ms deferral poll interval. The test verifies the exact same three behaviors as the L2 harness: deferral log fires, restart-pending log fires, and `stopChannel` / `startChannel` are never called.

- **Observed result after fix:** With `activeEmbeddedRunCount = 1` blocking the channel reload, the deferral loop starts (warn log). When `isGatewayRestartPending()` flips to `true` mid-poll, the loop bails (info log "channel reload deferred — skipping because gateway restart is now in progress"). The per-channel `restartChannel` for each `channelsToRestart` then sees the same `isGatewayRestartPending() = true` and skips with "skipping ${name} channel restart — gateway restart is in progress" — so `stopChannel` and `startChannel` are never called, and the in-process restart's own `startChannel` is the only one that runs.
- **Before evidence (revert replay):** Reverting the three `isGatewayRestartPending()` gates causes the new test to fail with `expected stopChannel not to have been called, but it was called with "discord"`. The deferral loop runs to the timeout, then `restartChannel` proceeds to `stopChannel("discord") + startChannel("discord")`, colliding with the in-process restart's `startChannel("discord")`. The crash loop is the same symptom the reporter observed in #79487.
- **What was not tested:** Live E2E EADDRINUSE scenario (requires a running gateway with a Telegram/Discord channel, triggering a config reload + gateway-tool restart in the deferral window). The local vitest test + this L2 runtime fixture verify the gate function behavior at the unit level and the deferral-loop interaction, which is the same decision surface the live path uses.
- **Proof limitations:** L2 runtime fixture (deterministic deferral timeline) + new vitest regression test. Live E2E crash-loop reproduction would constitute L3 but requires a 2-process setup (one issuing SIGUSR1 while another issues `config reload`).
- **Reproduction command for maintainers**:

  ```bash
  cd work/openclaw
  git checkout feat/issue-79487-channel-reload-race-deferred-reload
  node proof-79487-l2.mjs && \
    node --import tsx scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts
  ```

## Tests and validation

- `node proof-79487-l2.mjs` — exit 0, L2 fixture proves 3/3 verdicts
- `node --import tsx scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts` — full suite passed including the new regression test
- 1 new test added: `cancels deferred channel reload when gateway restart becomes pending during deferral polling`
- All 41 existing `server-reload-handlers.test.ts` tests still pass
- `pnpm check:changed` green for the touched surface (`src/infra/restart.ts`, `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts`, `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts`)

## Conflict resolution

The PR branch had a 3-way merge conflict in `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts` against `upstream/main`. The conflict was at the `createReloadHandlersForTest` helper signature, where `main` added an optional `channels?: { start, stop }` parameter that the PR's new test does not exercise.

**Resolution**: kept `main`'s `channels?` parameter (and its `channels?.start ?? vi.fn(...)` wiring inside the helper body) and added the PR's `vi.mock("../infra/restart.js", ...)` block before the helper definition. The new test uses `createGatewayReloadHandlers({...})` directly with its own `startChannel`/`stopChannel` mocks, so it does not depend on the `channels?` parameter — both the upstream test (which uses `createReloadHandlersForTest(undefined, channels)`) and the PR's new test work against the resolved file.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — for any user triggering a config reload during a SIGUSR1 in-process restart, the channel reload is now skipped instead of crashing the gateway in an EADDRINUSE loop. The in-process restart's own `startChannel` proceeds as normal.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
- **Highest-risk area:** The `isGatewayRestartPending()` check is called from three places (deferral start, deferral poll, `restartChannel`). If the function returns a stale value (e.g. a race where the restart signal has been consumed but the deferred reload's poll still sees `true`), the channel restart would be silently skipped. Mitigated by: (a) `isGatewayRestartPending()` is defined in `src/infra/restart.ts` and reads from the same `pendingRestartPreparing || hasUnconsumedRestartSignal()` state that the in-process restart consumes, so there is no stale-snapshot window in steady state, (b) the next non-restart reload attempt (e.g. a subsequent config change after the restart finishes) will see `false` and proceed normally.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Open for maintainer review
- **Live proof for maintainers:** A maintainer can apply `proof: override` if the L2 runtime fixture + the new vitest regression test is acceptable as the behavioral proof, or run the reproduction command above in a live 2-process setup (one issuing `kill -USR1 $(pidof openclaw)` while another issues `openclaw config reload`) to gather a live crash-loop transcript.

commands.review_summary:
The branch changes self-hosted provider discovery to pass `discoveryApiKey ?? apiKey` into `buildProvider` and adds regression tests for credential precedence, marker fallback, and missing keys.

PR surface: Source +1, Tests +86. Total +87 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. Current resolvers can expose a truthy public marker with `discoveryApiKey` undefined, and this PR would pass that marker into discovery code that builds bearer headers from any non-empty key.

target.title: fix(provider-catalog): marker-aware apiKey fallback for self-hosted discovery
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`discoverOpenAICompatibleSelfHostedProvider` was sending `GET /models` without
any `Authorization` header whenever the configured `apiKey` resolved to a
non-secret auth marker (e.g. `custom-local`, `ollama-local`, `VLLM_API_KEY`,
`secretref-managed`, `oauth:<provider>`). For auth-required self-hosted
endpoints (LiteLLM with `allow_user_auth: true`, vLLM, SGLang) this caused
401s and broke provider catalog discovery. For authless local servers the
gap was the underlying bug behind the symptom.

## Why This Change Was Made

The previous fix (#94473) tried to recover by unconditionally falling back
from `discoveryApiKey` to `apiKey`, but the fallback leaked the marker text
itself into the bearer header (`Bearer custom-local`, `Bearer VLLM_API_KEY`,
…). ClawSweeper flagged that as a correctness regression (P1, 0.94
confidence) — the bearer would not authenticate the request, and it
regressed any setup that intentionally relied on the marker not being
forwarded.

This rebase replaces the unconditional fallback with the same
marker-aware normalization the sibling live catalog helper already uses
(`src/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-live-runtime.ts:91` →
`normalizeLiveModelCatalogRequestApiKey`). The shared helper
`toDiscoveryApiKey` in `src/agents/models-config.providers.secret-helpers.ts`
filters out non-secret markers (env-var placeholders, OAuth markers,
SecretRef markers, local-auth sentinels, AWS SDK markers) and only
forwards concrete credentials. When only a marker is available, the
provider builder is invoked with no `apiKey` and downstream header
construction skips `Authorization` entirely.

The source change is one expression and one new import:

```ts
import { toDiscoveryApiKey } from "../agents/models-config.providers.secret-helpers.js";
…
const discoveryKey = discoveryApiKey ?? toDiscoveryApiKey(apiKey);
return {
  provider: {
    …(await params.buildProvider({
      apiKey: discoveryKey,
      …(configuredBaseUrl ? { baseUrl: configuredBaseUrl } : {}),
    })),
    apiKey,
  },
};
```

## User Impact

- Auth-required self-hosted providers (LiteLLM with auth, vLLM, SGLang)
  configured with an env-var-backed key now receive a real bearer.
- Authless local providers (Ollama, `custom-local`) stay authless — no
  placeholder text is sent.
- Existing setups that relied on `discoveryApiKey` continue to work
  unchanged (it is still preferred when present).

## Evidence

### L2 runtime proof (`node --import tsx proof-83461-l2.mjs`)

Captures the exact `(apiKey, baseUrl?)` payload passed to `buildProvider`
across eight representative inputs, plus the underlying
`toDiscoveryApiKey` filter outputs:

```text
=== AFTER fix (current source) ===

  1. both-set:           apiKey="OPENAI_API_KEY"  discoveryApiKey="sk-real-key-123" -> wire auth = Bearer sk-real-key-123, resultNull=false
  2. env-resolved-only:  apiKey="sk-real-key-456" discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = Bearer sk-real-key-456, resultNull=false
  3. local-marker:       apiKey="custom-local"     discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  4. env-var-marker:     apiKey="VLLM_API_KEY"     discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  5. secretref-marker:   apiKey="secretref-managed" discoveryApiKey=undefined        -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  6. oauth-marker:       apiKey="oauth:litellm"    discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  7. marker-with-discovery: apiKey="custom-local"  discoveryApiKey="sk-real-key-789" -> wire auth = Bearer sk-real-key-789, resultNull=false
  8. no-key:             apiKey=undefined          discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=true

=== toDiscoveryApiKey helper outputs (the marker filter) ===
  toDiscoveryApiKey("sk-real-key-456")    = "sk-real-key-456"
  toDiscoveryApiKey("custom-local")       = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("VLLM_API_KEY")       = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("secretref-managed")  = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("oauth:litellm")      = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("OPENAI_API_KEY")     = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("ollama-local")       = undefined
```

Verdicts:
- ✓ Concrete env-resolved key still wins (case 1, 2, 7).
- ✓ Non-secret markers are filtered, no `Authorization` header sent (3–6).
- ✓ No-key path returns `null` and never calls `buildProvider` (8).

### Unit test proof (`vitest run src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts`)

Eight focused tests cover the same matrix and pass locally:

```text
 ✓ src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts (18 tests) 9694ms
   … falls back to a concrete env-resolved apiKey when discoveryApiKey is undefined
   … forwards the resolver's discoveryApiKey to the provider builder when both are present
   … omits apiKey from the provider builder when apiKey is a non-secret local marker
   … omits apiKey when apiKey is an env-var placeholder marker (e.g. VLLM_API_KEY)
   … omits apiKey when apiKey is a non-env SecretRef marker
   … omits apiKey when apiKey is an OAuth marker (e.g. oauth:litellm)
   … prefers discoveryApiKey even when apiKey is a non-secret marker
   … returns null when apiKey is undefined
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
```

### What was not tested

- Live `GET /models` against a real LiteLLM / vLLM / SGLang instance
  (no compatible runtime available in this workspace; the marker
  classifier is the contract, not the HTTP layer).
- The CI job that was red on the previous version
  (`check-additional-boundaries-bcd`,
   `checks-node-core-runtime-infra-channel-plugin`) was failing on
  pre-existing main issues unrelated to this PR — those are being
  watched on this rebase to confirm the PR surface is now clean.

### Reproduction

```bash
git checkout fix/issue-83461-discovery-marker-aware   # or feat/issue-83461-… after rebase
node --import tsx proof-83461-l2.mjs
node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts \
  src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts
```

## Files changed

1. `src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.ts` — replace
   `apiKey: discoveryApiKey` with
   `apiKey: discoveryApiKey ?? toDiscoveryApiKey(apiKey)`, plus the
   matching import.
2. `src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts` — 8 focused tests
   covering discoveryApiKey precedence, env-resolved concrete keys, the
   four non-secret marker classes, no-key, and the
   `discoveryApiKey`-over-marker precedence rule.

commands.author: xydt-tanshanshan

target.title: fix(compaction): preflight compaction uses configured compaction timeout instead of reply operation abort signal
target.body:
## Summary

Preflight (budget-triggered) compaction received `replyOperation.abortSignal`, causing `compactWithSafetyTimeout`'s `Promise.race` to kill slow compactions at ~60s — the reply lifecycle timeout — ignoring `compaction.timeoutSeconds`. The fix introduces a cancellation-only signal that forwards explicit user abort and gateway restart but filters out the lifecycle timeout, letting `compactWithSafetyTimeout` be the sole timeout owner.

- **Problem**: `replyOperation.abortSignal` fires on three triggers: (1) user abort, (2) gateway restart, (3) lifecycle timeout at ~60s. Preflight compaction should cancel on (1) and (2) but not on (3), yet all three fire the same `AbortController`. Slow compactions on large sessions never complete.
- **Solution**: Create `createPreflightCompactionCancelSignal(replyOperation)` that inspects `abortSignal.reason.name`: propagate for user abort / restart, suppress for `TimeoutError` (lifecycle timeout). `compactWithSafetyTimeout` handles the 180s default timeout.
- **What changed**: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts` — +17 lines: new `createPreflightCompactionCancelSignal` helper + use it at the compaction call site. `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts` — +79 lines: 3 tests (1 integration + 2 direct unit tests).
- **What did NOT change**: Memory flush path's `runEmbeddedAgent` still uses `replyOperation.abortSignal` directly. Non-preflight compaction paths. `compactWithSafetyTimeout` core logic. `resolveCompactionTimeoutMs`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #95553
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

OpenClaw runs two compaction paths with different effective timeouts:

| Path | Trigger | Effective timeout | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overflow recovery | `trigger=overflow` | ~180s | `resolveCompactionTimeoutMs(cfg)` via `compactWithSafetyTimeout` |
| **Preflight / budget** | `trigger=budget` | **~60s** | `replyOperation.abortSignal` (lifecycle timeout, NOT config-derived) |

The preflight path fires before every turn where `totalTokens >= contextWindow - reserveTokensFloor - softThresholdTokens`. On backends where summarizing a large context takes >60s, preflight compaction can never complete. The reply lifecycle timeout (`TimeoutError`) from `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts` aborts the controller, and `compactWithSafetyTimeout`'s `Promise.race` kills the compaction.

The issue reporter observed: 24 of 26 incomplete compactions clustered at exactly 60–61s, while successful compactions had median duration 278s.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Preflight compaction cancel signal no longer carries the reply lifecycle timeout, allowing compactions to run up to the configured `compaction.timeoutSeconds`.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19+, vitest
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts --run
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts --run
 Tests  49 passed (49)
```

| Scenario | abortSignal.reason.name | Cancel signal outcome |
|---|---|---|
| User abort | `AbortError` | ✅ Cancelled |
| Gateway restart | `AbortError` (code: `OPENCLAW_RESTART_ABORT`) | ✅ Cancelled |
| Lifecycle timeout (~60s) | `TimeoutError` | ❌ Not cancelled — compaction continues to config timeout |

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. Preflight compaction receives a new cancel signal (not raw `replyOperation.abortSignal`) — verified by reference inequality test
  2. User abort propagates through the cancel signal — verified by unit test
  3. Lifecycle `TimeoutError` (reason name) does not propagate — verified by unit test
  4. All 46 existing tests in `agent-runner-memory.test.ts` continue to pass

- **What was not tested**: Live end-to-end verification with slow compaction backends (vLLM/Qwen). Unit tests prove the signal filtering logic; real-world 60s→278s completion improvement requires a slow-backend deployment.

## Root Cause

**Provenance**: `introduced by` commit `5374c7a8a` (Peter Steinberger, 2026-05-30) which created both `compactWithSafetyTimeout` (with `Promise.race` against external abort) and `createReplyOperation` (with `AbortController`). The preflight path connected them via commit `280d1cb97` (Vincent Koc, 2026-06-09).

**Data flow**: `agent-runner.ts` → `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` → `compactEmbeddedAgentSession` → `compactWithSafetyTimeout` → `Promise.race([compactPromise, externalAbortPromise])`. The lifecycle timeout fires via `src/gateway/server-maintenance.ts:284` (`setInterval 60s`) → `chat-abort.ts:476` (`abort("timeout")`) → `replyOperation.abortSignal` → `Promise.race` kills compaction.

**Confidence**: `clear` — source inspection confirms the signal chain, unit tests validate the filtering logic.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Preflight compaction on slow backends can now complete within the configured `compaction.timeoutSeconds` (default 180s) instead of being killed at ~60s by the lifecycle timeout.
- User abort and gateway restart still cancel preflight compaction.
- No config changes required.

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios**: User abort propagates through cancel signal; lifecycle TimeoutError is filtered out; existing 46 tests pass
- **Edge cases checked**: Pre-aborted signal (already aborted before `createPreflightCompactionCancelSignal` is called); abort reason inspection
- **What you did NOT verify**: Live slow-backend deployment showing 60s→278s completion improvement

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — existing config keys unchanged
- [ ] Config/env changes? No
- [ ] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. The fix identifies the correct boundary — distinguishing lifecycle timeout from explicit cancellation via `abortSignal.reason.name` — and creates a per-call cancellation-only signal. This preserves the explicit cancel contract (stop/restart) while removing the lifecycle timeout race. `compactWithSafetyTimeout` is the sole timeout owner.
- **Refactor needed**: No.
- **Alternative considered**: Passing no abort signal at all. Rejected — loses explicit stop/restart cancellation, which the review specifically asked to preserve. Composing with `AbortSignal.any` + `AbortSignal.timeout`. Rejected — duplicates timeout ownership and the reply signal can still win first.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **AI model**: deepseek-v4-pro
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest risk area**: The `abortSignal.reason` inspection assumes `TimeoutError` name reliably identifies lifecycle timeout vs other timeout errors. Validated against `chat-abort.ts:476` which creates `reason.name = "TimeoutError"`.
- **Mitigation**: The filter is narrow — only `TimeoutError` is suppressed. Any other Error (including provider-level timeouts) propagates through and cancels compaction.

target.title: fix(cron): preserve isolated success on delivery failure
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve successful isolated cron agent runs when the post-run delivery phase returns an error.
- Keep delivery failure metadata (`error`, `delivered`, `deliveryAttempted`, and diagnostics) so cron state can record `not-delivered` separately from agent execution status.
- Add a regression test for a successful final report followed by a delivery dispatcher failure.

Fixes #94058

## Root Cause

`finalizeCronRun` propagated a `dispatchCronDelivery` result with `status: "error"` directly when the agent payload itself was non-fatal. That collapsed delivery/finalization failures into the isolated agent execution result, so the outer scheduled run could report `status=error` even though the isolated session had ended successfully.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts src/cron/service.runs-one-shot-main-job-disables-it.test.ts src/cron/service.persists-delivered-status.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Scheduled isolated `agentTurn` jobs could produce a successful isolated session/final assistant message, but a post-run delivery error caused the outer cron run to persist `status=error`, making the success look like an agent failure.
- **Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw source checkout at head `291dc8e6896c8cced2df1b7e5f5b5da0e100904d`, with dependencies hydrated by `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` and commands run from the repository root.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the focused cron runner/service regression command, core typecheck, repo formatter check, and whitespace diff check listed in Validation after applying this patch.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the local OpenClaw source checkout after the patch:

  ```text
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts src/cron/service.runs-one-shot-main-job-disables-it.test.ts src/cron/service.persists-delivered-status.test.ts
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts

   RUN  v4.1.8 /Users/stellarfish/.openmeta/worktrees/openclaw__openclaw/openmeta__94058-cron-isolated-outcome

   Test Files  5 passed (5)
        Tests  155 passed (155)
     Start at  19:30:54
     Duration  15.08s (transform 1.90s, setup 299ms, import 3.94s, tests 10.65s, environment 0ms)

  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 47.11s

  $ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
  # exited 0

  $ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts
  Checking formatting...

  All matched files use the correct format.
  Finished in 12ms on 2 files using 1 threads.

  $ git diff --check
  # exited 0
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The new regression path verifies `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn` keeps the isolated run result at `status: "ok"` when post-run delivery fails, while preserving `error: "Message failed"`, `delivered: false`, `deliveryAttempted: true`, and a `source: "delivery"` diagnostic so the outer cron service can record delivery failure separately.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a live scheduled gateway/cron delivery against a real chat provider; the after-fix proof is local source-checkout terminal output plus focused runner/service regression coverage.

target.title: fix(tools): surface empty availability group diagnostics via onHiddenDiagnostic callback
target.body:
## Summary

- Empty `allOf`/`anyOf` availability groups are authoring errors, but diagnostics were silently consumed by `buildToolPlan` — tools hidden from the plan with no observable signal.
- Adds `onHiddenDiagnostic` callback to `BuildToolPlanOptions`, wires planner diagnostics into `resolvePluginTools`, and preserves plugin tool `availability` expressions in `capturePluginToolDescriptor`.
- Strengthens `isToolAvailabilityExpression` with recursive array validation so non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values are rejected at the capture boundary instead of crashing plugin tool resolution.

## Root Cause (full chain)

Symptom: Plugin/core authors define `availability: { anyOf: [] }` (empty group) and the tool silently disappears from the plan with no warning.

Layer 1: `evaluateExpression` in `availability.ts` detects empty `allOf`/`anyOf` arrays and returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostics, but `buildToolPlan` receives them and hides the tool with no callback or logging path.

Layer 2: No mechanism exists for callers to observe planner diagnostics. The `unsupported-signal` reason code marks authoring errors that should be surfaced, but there is no callback, logger, or event emitter.

Layer 3 (crash path, added by this PR's original non-array validation gap): `isToolAvailabilityExpression` only checked for key *presence* (`"allOf" in obj` / `"anyOf" in obj`) without validating that the values are actually arrays. A JS plugin returning `availability: { allOf: "not-array" }` would pass through descriptor capture and reach `evaluateExpression`, which calls `expression.allOf.flatMap(...)` on a string — crashing plugin tool resolution.

Root cause: The planner has no channel to surface authoring errors, and the availability shape guard did not validate group field types, allowing a crash path through descriptor capture → audit → evaluateExpression.

Missing guardrail: No recursive array validation in the expression shape guard, and no defensive Array.isArray check in evaluateExpression.

## Fix

- `src/tools/availability.ts`: Rewrote `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` with recursive validation — checks that `allOf`/`anyOf` values are arrays and their entries are valid expressions. Added defensive `Array.isArray` checks in `evaluateExpression` allOf/anyOf paths as a safety net.
- `src/tools/types.ts`: Added `onHiddenDiagnostic` to `BuildToolPlanOptions`.
- `src/tools/planner.ts`: Calls `onHiddenDiagnostic` for each diagnostic that hides a tool.
- `src/agents/tools/common.ts`: Declared `availability?: ToolAvailabilityExpression` on `AnyAgentTool`.
- `src/plugin-sdk/{index,core,plugin-entry}.ts`: Exported `ToolAvailabilityExpression` and `ToolAvailabilitySignal`.
- `src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts`: Preserves typed `availability` expressions with `isToolAvailabilityExpression` guard.
- `src/plugins/tools.ts`: Runs plugin-authored availability through `buildToolPlan` during `resolvePluginTools`, logs malformed authoring diagnostics, and returns only planner-visible plugin tools.
- Tests: planner, descriptor cache, and `resolvePluginTools` integration coverage.

## What did NOT change (scope boundary)

- `evaluateToolAvailability` and `evaluateExpression` signatures are unchanged.
- The `ToolPlan.hidden` array still contains diagnostics (backward compatible).
- This PR intentionally does not distinguish between runtime availability diagnostics and authoring errors — all hidden-tool diagnostics are surfaced uniformly.
- The recursive validation in `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` is purely additive — it rejects more invalid shapes but accepts all valid expressions that the old guard accepted.

## Compatibility

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Valid expression `{ allOf: [{kind:"always"}] }` | Captured, evaluated | Same as before |
| Non-array `{ allOf: "not-array" }` | Captured → crash in evaluateExpression | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| Non-array `{ anyOf: "not-array" }` | Captured → crash in evaluateExpression | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| `{ allOf: [{notKind:true}] }` (non-expression entries) | Captured → evaluated as unsupported | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| Empty `{ allOf: [] }` | Captured → unsupported-signal diagnostic | Same as before |
| Non-expression metadata `{ privateMeta: true }` | Rejected (no kind/allOf/anyOf) | Same as before |
| `evaluateToolAvailability` called directly with non-array | Crash (`.flatMap is not a function`) | Returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostic |

This is purely additive — no existing valid call path is affected. Non-array values that previously crashed are now either rejected at the guard or return a diagnostic.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` availability values could pass through `isToolAvailabilityExpression`, get captured in plugin tool descriptors, and crash during `resolvePluginTools` → `buildToolPlan` → `evaluateExpression` with `.flatMap is not a function`.

**Environment tested:** Node v22.22.0 on Linux, branch `fix/92361-tool-availability-empty-group-warning`, head `92f95538b36673e7005158a4b55883500522c000`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { isToolAvailabilityExpression } from './src/tools/availability.js';

const cases = [
  ['valid allOf', { allOf: [{ kind: 'always' }] }],
  ['non-array allOf', { allOf: 'not-array' }],
  ['non-array anyOf', { anyOf: 'not-array' }],
  ['allOf non-expr entries', { allOf: [{ notKind: true }] }],
  ['anyOf non-expr entries', { anyOf: [{ notKind: true }] }],
];
for (const [label, val] of cases) {
  const result = isToolAvailabilityExpression(val as any);
  console.log(label + ': ' + result);
}
"
```

Crash-prevention proof:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { evaluateToolAvailability } from './src/tools/availability.js';
const badDescriptor = {
  name: 'bad', description: 'bad', inputSchema: { type: 'object' },
  owner: { kind: 'plugin', pluginId: 'test' },
  executor: { kind: 'plugin', pluginId: 'test', toolName: 'bad' },
  availability: { allOf: 'not-array' },
};
try {
  const diags = evaluateToolAvailability({ descriptor: badDescriptor as any });
  console.log('no crash, diags=' + JSON.stringify(diags));
} catch(e) {
  console.log('CRASHED: ' + (e as Error).message.substring(0, 80));
}
"

```

**Evidence after fix:**

isToolAvailabilityExpression validation:

```text
valid allOf: true
non-array allOf: false
non-array anyOf: false
allOf non-expr entries: false
anyOf non-expr entries: false
```

evaluateToolAvailability with non-array allOf:

```text
no crash, diags=[{"reason":"unsupported-signal","message":"Unsupported availability expression"}]
```

resolvePluginTools with empty anyOf:

```text
empty group tools=
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Before/after comparison (old guard vs new recursive validation):

```text
=== BEFORE (af05d19d3c6 - old guard) ===
isToolAvailabilityExpression({ allOf: "not-array" }) = true
-> Non-array allOf PASSES the guard -> reaches capturePluginToolDescriptor
-> resolvePluginTools -> buildToolPlan -> evaluateExpression
-> expression.allOf.flatMap() THROWS: .flatMap is not a function
-> RESULT: plugin tool resolution crashes for malformed descriptors

=== AFTER (461e44e2b2 - recursive validation) ===
isToolAvailabilityExpression({ allOf: "not-array" }) = false
-> Non-array allOf REJECTED by guard -> NOT captured
-> capturePluginToolDescriptor returns availability: undefined
-> RESULT: plugin tool resolution is safe

evaluateToolAvailability(non-array allOf) = [{"reason":"unsupported-signal"...}]
Defensive Array.isArray guard in evaluateExpression: ACTIVE
```

**Observed result after fix:** `isToolAvailabilityExpression` rejects non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values and non-expression entries inside them. `evaluateExpression` returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostics for non-array values that bypass the type system. `resolvePluginTools` filters hidden plugin tools and logs malformed authoring diagnostics. The empty-group authoring warning for `{ anyOf: [] }` is preserved.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway startup against a real external plugin package with malformed availability metadata. Deliberate ESLint/type system bypass at the JS plugin boundary in production.

## Regression Test Proof

Tests pass on the fix branch (head `461e44e2b2`):

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
```

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  95 passed (95)
```

New regression tests added:
- `tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`: "rejects availability allOf that is not an array", "rejects availability anyOf that is not an array", "rejects availability allOf with non-expression entries"
- `tools.optional.test.ts`: covers empty/malformed availability diagnostics and plugin-authored env/config/auth availability enforcement.

Without the fix, the old `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` (key-presence check only) accepts `{ allOf: "not-array" }` as a valid expression shape, allowing it through descriptor capture into `evaluateExpression` where `.flatMap()` throws:

```text
TypeError: expression.allOf.flatMap is not a function
    at evaluateExpression (src/tools/availability.ts:149:31)
    at buildToolPlan (src/tools/planner.ts:49:7)
```

## Verification

- `npx vitest run src/tools/planner.test.ts` — 8 passed
- `npx vitest run src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts` — 9 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` — 78 passed
- `git diff --check` — clean

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No
- Fail open → fail closed: Non-array availability values are now rejected (fail-closed) rather than causing crashes. The `evaluateExpression` defensive checks return unsupported-signal diagnostics instead of throwing, preventing denial-of-service via malformed plugin descriptors.

Closes #92361.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15)

Head after follow-up: `84f9a1b1cc24ceb107bbbbdbb816d801514ad713`.

ClawSweeper asked for the public SDK contract to be documented or confined. This follow-up documents plugin tool `availability` in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`, including the exported `ToolAvailabilityExpression` type, supported signals, `allOf`/`anyOf` composition, and the rule that empty/malformed expressions are authoring errors logged during runtime tool resolution.

It also fixes the red CI expectation in `src/tools/planner.test.ts`: unknown signal shapes are now rejected by the expression-level guard, so the planner diagnostic is `Unsupported availability expression`.

Additional registration-path proof after the latest signal-validation changes:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings --eval '<script imports resolvePluginTools, installs an active proof plugin registry + manifest snapshot, registers proof_tool with availability: { anyOf: [] }, and calls resolvePluginTools({ toolAllowlist: ["proof_tool"] })>'
```

Observed terminal output:

```text
resolvePluginTools returned tools=proof_tool
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Fresh verification on this follow-up:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       8 passed (8)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       87 passed (87)

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

Docs validation note: `pnpm docs:check-mdx` could not start because pnpm dependency status invoked `node-llama-cpp` postinstall, which requires CMake 3.19+; this runner has CMake 3.11.4. No MDX parser failure was reached.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15, availability enforcement)

Head after enforcement follow-up: `c83d47b7c8`.

This follow-up addresses the ClawSweeper patch-quality findings from the prior review:

- Enforces plugin-authored `availability` before returning tools from `resolvePluginTools`. The resolver now builds a planner result from captured descriptors and only returns visible tools; hidden runtime `auth`, `config`, and `env` gates fail closed.
- Preserves malformed availability-like records through descriptor capture so malformed `kind`, `allOf`, or `anyOf` values reach the planner/audit path and emit authoring warnings instead of being silently dropped.
- Fixes the `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` type export path by exporting `ToolAvailabilityExpression` and `ToolAvailabilitySignal` from `../tools/types.js`.

Additional non-test registration-path proof after enforcement:

```text
empty group tools=
env satisfied tools=proof_tool
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Fresh verification:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       95 passed (95)

node --import tsx --no-warnings --eval "import type { ToolAvailabilityExpression } from './src/plugin-sdk/core.ts'; const expr: ToolAvailabilityExpression = { kind: 'always' }; console.log('core export ok: ' + expr.kind);"
core export ok: always

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

`pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` is still blocked before running the API check by local dependency status invoking `node-llama-cpp` postinstall, which requires CMake 3.19+ while this runner has CMake 3.11.4. The direct `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` import smoke above covers the reported broken export path on this checkout.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15, dead path cleanup)

Head after dependency-check cleanup: `198739f1b177b4559e176503b522be3ce1b3bd71`.

The enforcement follow-up made the standalone plugin descriptor audit helper obsolete: `src/plugins/tools.ts` now owns the runtime planner call that filters hidden plugin tools and logs authoring diagnostics. This follow-up deletes the unused `src/plugins/tool-descriptor-plan-audit.ts` helper and its now-isolated test so dependency/dead-code checks only see the active runtime path.

Fresh local verification:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       95 passed (95)

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

Local `pnpm deadcode:*` commands are still blocked before running by dependency status invoking `node-llama-cpp` postinstall on this machine; the installed CMake is 3.11.4 and `node-llama-cpp` requires CMake 3.19+. The PR CI `check-dependencies` run on GitHub's Ubuntu runner is the dependency-check proof for this head.

target.body:
## Summary
- Thread cron session metadata into before-tool-call hook context for embedded cron agent runs.
- Downgrade non-critical loop-warning diagnostics to `info` only for fresh `sessionTarget="isolated"` cron sessions, while preserving warning/critical behavior for other runs.
- Add focused coverage for isolated cron repeat warnings emitting `info` diagnostics.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: focused Tool Search Vitest file, targeted oxlint, diff whitespace check, and branch autoreview commands listed above.
