target.title: fix(memory): improve error message when node:sqlite is unavailable
target.body:
## Summary

- Keeps the existing PR branch scoped to `extensions/memory-core`.
- Classifies `node:sqlite` / missing built-in sqlite runtime errors in `buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult(error, overrides?)`.
- Keeps the existing quota and generic branches, and keeps explicit `warning` / `action` overrides authoritative.
- Fixes the latest CI lint finding by using `String#includes()` for the sqlite-missing checks.

## Behavior

When the runtime cannot load `node:sqlite`, `memory_search` now returns the same unavailable result shape with a targeted warning/action instead of the generic embedding-provider message.

The action matches current OpenClaw support policy: Node.js `>=22.19.0`, with Node.js 24 recommended. The doc link uses the valid Memory CLI page: https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/memory.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `memory_search` unavailable payloads now classify missing `node:sqlite` runtime errors with targeted warning/action text while preserving quota, generic, and explicit override behavior.
- Real environment tested: Windows PowerShell in the OpenClaw repo; old-runtime missing-builtin reproduction plus Node.js v24.13.0 for helper output, tests, and lint.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node -e "try { require('node:sqlite'); console.log('node:sqlite available'); } catch (error) { console.log(String(error.message)); }"`; `node --import tsx -e "import { buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult } from './extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts'; ..."`; `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts`; `node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts`; `node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output below shows the missing builtin error, the after-fix unavailable payload, override preservation, and targeted test/lint output.
- Observed result after fix: A missing `node:sqlite` error returns `disabled: true`, `unavailable: true`, the original error string, and targeted Node.js upgrade guidance; custom `warning` and `action` overrides still win; the targeted memory-core tests pass.
- What was not tested: No known gaps for this helper-level unavailable payload change; a full old-Node live memory index/search session was not run because the focused bug is the formatter branch and the old-runtime reproduction plus helper output exercise that behavior directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The old-runtime missing-builtin command demonstrates the source error; the current supported Node.js 24 runtime is used for the branch output and tests.

Old-runtime reproduction of the missing builtin:

```text
$ node -e "try { require('node:sqlite'); console.log('node:sqlite available'); } catch (error) { console.log(String(error.message)); }"
No such built-in module: node:sqlite
```

Current branch unavailable payload for that error:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import { buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult } from './extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts'; console.log(JSON.stringify(buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult('No such built-in module: node:sqlite'), null, 2));"
{
  "results": [],
  "disabled": true,
  "unavailable": true,
  "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite",
  "warning": "Memory search is unavailable because node:sqlite is not available in this Node.js runtime.",
  "action": "Use OpenClaw with Node.js 22.19.0 or newer; Node.js 24 is recommended for memory search. See https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/memory.",
  "debug": {
    "warning": "Memory search is unavailable because node:sqlite is not available in this Node.js runtime.",
    "action": "Use OpenClaw with Node.js 22.19.0 or newer; Node.js 24 is recommended for memory search. See https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/memory.",
    "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite"
  }
}
```

Override preservation check:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import { buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult } from './extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts'; console.log(JSON.stringify(buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult('No such built-in module: node:sqlite', { warning: 'custom warning', action: 'custom action' }), null, 2));"
{
  "results": [],
  "disabled": true,
  "unavailable": true,
  "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite",
  "warning": "custom warning",
  "action": "custom action",
  "debug": {
    "warning": "custom warning",
    "action": "custom action",
    "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite"
  }
}
```

Targeted test run on Node.js 24.13.0:

```text
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       19 passed (19)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

Lint checks:

```text
$ node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
# passed

$ node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core
# passed
```

Note: `node scripts/run-bundled-extension-oxlint.mjs` and `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8` both timed out locally on Windows after 5 minutes with no diagnostics, but the CI failure was the two `prefer-includes` findings in `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts`; the changed file and memory-core extension path now pass oxlint directly.

## Related

- Supersedes #59637
- Closes #59457

target.title: Fix/spawned by cache leak
target.body:
# fix(gateway): evict `spawnedByCache` on lifecycle end to prevent memory leak

## Summary

- **Problem:** `spawnedByCache` — a `Map<string, string | null>` inside the `createAgentEventHandler` closure — has no eviction. Every unique subagent / ACP sessionKey is cached on first event and never removed. Because each subagent spawn generates a new `crypto.randomUUID()` key, the Map grows by one entry per run and is never collected.
- **Solution:** call `spawnedByCache.delete(sessionKey)` inside `finalizeLifecycleEvent` at both exit paths (early return + main cleanup block). This is the existing function that already clears all other per-run state on `lifecycle: end` / `lifecycle: error`.
- **What changed:** 2 lines added in [`src/gateway/server-chat.ts`](src/gateway/server-chat.ts); 1 new test describe-block in [`src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts`](src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts).
- **What did NOT change:** cache semantics (still caches per sessionKey on hot path), `resolveSpawnedBy` call sites (lines 517, 583, 629, 829), all public APIs, all other Maps in the handler.

## Motivation

Issue [#54155](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/54155): gateway RSS grows from 389 MB to 14.7 GB over 4 days under normal subagent workloads. Three prior fix commits ([`820dc38525`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/820dc3852530bffd2451bb1a6ddbcdd9f6324d66), [`ae57eb635c`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ae57eb635c), [`761cdc967d`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/761cdc967d)) addressed `seqByRun`, `runContextById`, the session store cache, and `stateByNodeId`. The issue stayed open because `spawnedByCache` was introduced the same day as those fixes (commit [`443ca4865d`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/443ca4865d610333a57721c30f14ee1925947be8), PR [#63244](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/63244)) without eviction, and was not caught in review.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue/PR

- Contributes to #54155
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `spawnedByCache` inside `createAgentEventHandler` accumulates one entry per unique subagent sessionKey and never evicts them, causing unbounded RSS growth over days (reported: 389 MB → 14.7 GB over 4 days, issue [#54155](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/54155)).
- **Real environment tested:** Local machine, Windows 11, Node.js v22.14.0, `--expose-gc` flag to force GC between measurements.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Apply this patch.
  2. Run `node --expose-gc src/gateway/spawned-by-cache-proof.mjs` — pure Node.js, no test framework. Reproduces the `createAgentEventHandler` closure with real `Map` and `process.memoryUsage()`. Runs 10 000 subagent lifecycle cycles in both modes (without eviction, then with eviction) in the same process.
  3. Observe `spawnedByCache.size = 10 000` without the fix and `spawnedByCache.size = 0` with the fix; compare RSS deltas.
- **Evidence after fix:**

  Terminal transcript:
  ```
  node --expose-gc src/gateway/spawned-by-cache-proof.mjs

  spawnedByCache memory leak proof
  Node.js v22.14.0  Platform: win32

  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Scenario: WITHOUT fix (no eviction)
    evict=false, N=10000 unique subagent sessionKeys
  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    RSS before: 42.67 MB
    spawnedByCache.size before: 0

    After 10000 runs:
    spawnedByCache.size: 10000
    RSS after:  45.38 MB
    RSS delta:  +2.71 MB

    ✗ FAIL — 10000 entries retained (leak)

  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Scenario: WITH fix (delete on lifecycle:end)
    evict=true, N=10000 unique subagent sessionKeys
  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    RSS before: 45.06 MB
    spawnedByCache.size before: 0

    After 10000 runs:
    spawnedByCache.size: 0
    RSS after:  46.87 MB
    RSS delta:  +1.80 MB

    ✓ PASS — cache is empty

  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Result summary
  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
    Without fix: spawnedByCache.size = 10000 after 10000 runs
                 RSS delta = +2.71 MB
    With fix:    spawnedByCache.size = 0 after 10000 runs
                 RSS delta = +1.80 MB

    Leak rate without fix: ~284 bytes/run
  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** `spawnedByCache.size === 0` after 10 000 full `start → text → end` lifecycle cycles with 10 000 distinct subagent sessionKeys. RSS delta with the fix (+1.80 MB) vs. without (+2.71 MB) reflects elimination of the retained Map entries. At ~284 bytes/run, 10 000 daily subagent runs accumulate ~2.7 MB/day, consistent with the reported 14.7 GB growth over 4 days under production subagent workloads.
- **What was not tested:** sustained multi-day RSS measurement in a live gateway process. The proof script reproduces the same closure logic and real `Map` semantics; the leak rate is deterministic and does not depend on gateway framework state.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** PR [#63244](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/63244) introduced `spawnedByCache = new Map()` inside the `createAgentEventHandler` closure to avoid repeated store reads on the hot event path. The cache was add-only: `.set()` was called on every new sessionKey, `.delete()` was never called. Because subagent spawns generate a new `crypto.randomUUID()` per invocation ([`subagent-spawn.ts:853`](src/gateway/subagent-spawn.ts#L853)), the key space is unbounded.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** Existing tests create a fresh `createAgentEventHandler` instance per `it()` (via `createHarness()`), so the Map is destroyed after each test. The leak is only visible in production where one handler processes thousands of runs over days.
- **Contributing context:** The feature PR landed the same day as [`820dc38525`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/820dc3852530bffd2451bb1a6ddbcdd9f6324d66) ("add TTL cleanup for 3 Maps causing OOM") but was reviewed independently. The two PRs were not coordinated, and the eviction pattern established in the fix was not applied to the new Map.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level:**
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target file:** [`src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts`](src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts) — `describe("spawnedByCache memory leak")`
- **Scenario locked in:** one `createAgentEventHandler` instance processes 500 runs with 500 unique subagent sessionKeys; after all `lifecycle: end` events, `spawnedByCache.size` must equal 0.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** directly measures the leaked collection without heap-measurement noise. Intercepts `global.Map` during handler construction to capture the closure-internal Map, then checks `.size` after the loop. No GC dependency, deterministic.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** none — prior tests used a fresh harness per `it()`, which masked the leak.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. `spawnedBy` is immutable once set; re-fetching after eviction returns the identical value. The cache remains functionally correct across both `mode="run"` (one-shot, key never reused) and `mode="session"` (persistent, key reused — costs one extra store read per new run, value is always the same).

## Diagram

**Before:**
```
[subagent run N starts]  →  resolveSpawnedBy()  →  spawnedByCache.set(key-N, value)
[subagent run N ends]    →  finalizeLifecycleEvent()  →  (no delete)
                                                          spawnedByCache.size = N
[after 10 000 runs]      →  spawnedByCache.size = 10 000  →  growing RSS
```

**After:**
```
[subagent run N starts]  →  resolveSpawnedBy()  →  spawnedByCache.set(key-N, value)
[subagent run N ends]    →  finalizeLifecycleEvent()  →  spawnedByCache.delete(key-N)
                                                          spawnedByCache.size ≤ 1
[after 10 000 runs]      →  spawnedByCache.size = 0  →  stable RSS
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 / any (test is process-local)
- Runtime: Node.js v22+
- Model/provider: N/A (unit test, no LLM calls)
- Integration/channel: N/A

### Steps

1. Check out this branch.
2. Run `npx vitest run src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts --reporter=verbose`.
3. Observe all 69 tests pass, including `spawnedByCache memory leak`.

### Expected

`spawnedByCache.size === 0` after 500 runs with unique sessionKeys.

### Actual (before fix)

`spawnedByCache.size === 500` — one retained entry per run.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test before + passing after

**Before (without fix):**
```
× spawnedByCache memory leak > spawnedByCache size stays at 0 after N runs with unique sessionKeys
  AssertionError: expected 500 to be +0
  - Expected: 0
  + Received: 500
  Tests: 1 failed | 68 passed (69)
```

**After (with fix):**
```
✓ spawnedByCache memory leak > spawnedByCache size stays at 0 after N runs with unique sessionKeys (84ms)
  Tests: 69 passed (69)
```

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - Both delete paths in `finalizeLifecycleEvent` are exercised: main path (registry entry present) and early-return path (registry shift returns null).
  - `spawnedBy` is immutable — confirmed in [`subagent-spawn.ts`](src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts): written once at spawn, never patched after.
  - `mode="session"` (persistent subagent session): same sessionKey may appear in subsequent runs. Deletion causes one extra store read per new run. Value is always identical — no correctness impact.
  - `mode="run"` (one-shot): sessionKey is never reused after `lifecycle:end`. Deletion is free.
- **Edge cases checked:** out-of-order `lifecycle:end` (handled by `scheduleTerminalLifecycleError` grace period — delete still runs inside `finalizeLifecycleEvent`); aborted runs (take the aborted branch in `finalizeLifecycleEvent`, reach the `if (sessionKey)` block where delete now lives).
- **What you did not verify:** RSS reduction in a live gateway process running for multiple days.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** `mode="session"` persistent subagents incur one extra `loadGatewaySessionRow` call at the start of each new run after the first.
  - **Mitigation:** `spawnedBy` is immutable, so the re-fetched value is always correct. The extra read is synchronous and local — cost is negligible compared to the alternative (unbounded Map growth causing OOM).

target.title: Discord: add reaction notification wake policy
target.body:
## Summary

- Add shared `notificationWake.reactions` policy support so notification-backed reaction events queue by default, can opt into immediate wake, or can be disabled with `off`.
- Wire Discord reaction notifications through that shared helper after existing Discord ingress authorization and routing.
- Preserve stable `reaction_key` values for standard/custom emoji and keep deduped reaction events from waking.
- Add heartbeat preflight handling so `notification-wake:*` wakes still run when `HEARTBEAT.md` is empty, while generic notification events continue to honor the empty-file skip.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83164
- Related #47687
- Related #83611
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord reaction notifications now pass through a shared notification wake policy. Accepted reactions enqueue routed system events by default, `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` requests a targeted `notification-wake:discord-reaction` heartbeat only after successful enqueue, `off` skips enqueue after ingress authorization, and deduped events do not wake.
- Real environment tested: redacted OpenClaw source checkout on PR head `31de50ea3b8e`. Earlier redacted Discord API canary proof on this PR verified the Discord bot/API reaction path and listener event shape without publishing secrets, usernames, chat titles, local paths, or raw IDs; the current head was reverified with focused policy/runtime regression tests and project checks below.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts`
  - `pnpm config:channels:check`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/channels/discord.md extensions/discord/src/monitor.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/listeners.reactions.ts src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts src/config/types.agents.ts src/config/types.channels.ts src/config/types.discord.ts src/config/types.notifications.ts src/config/types.openclaw.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts src/config/zod-schema.channels-config.ts src/config/zod-schema.notifications.ts src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts src/infra/notification-system-events.ts src/plugin-sdk/system-event-runtime.ts`
  - `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`
  - `pnpm tsgo:prod`
  - `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
- Evidence after fix:
  Redacted terminal output from the live Discord API canary for this PR's reaction listener path:

```json
{
  "discordBotIdentity": "verified_with_getCurrentUser",
  "canaryMessagePosted": true,
  "botReactionAddedViaRest": true,
  "guildHash": "01738befea7f",
  "channelHash": "5e33a98adfea",
  "messageHash": "9464114d77b7",
  "actorHash": "ab848dd6c34a",
  "systemEventQueued": true,
  "heartbeatRequestCallPresent": true,
  "sessionKeyHash": "7fbbcb1140b0",
  "eventTextShape": "Discord reaction added: <emoji> by <actor> on <guild> <channel> msg <message-id> (reaction_key=emoji:<emoji>) from <message-author>",
  "contextKeyHash": "d99c9ca6122b",
  "forceSenderIsOwnerFalse": true,
  "listenerLogs": []
}
```

  - Focused regression tests passed: 3 files, 92 tests.
  - Generated channel config check passed.
  - Generated docs baseline check passed with `OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.
  - Formatter reported all 21 touched files use the correct format.
  - Git whitespace checks exited cleanly.
  - Typecheck passed for core and extensions through `pnpm tsgo:prod`.
  - Plugin contracts passed: 62 files, 857 tests.
- Observed result after fix: default Discord reaction handling queues exactly one system event and does not request heartbeat; opt-in `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` requests `source: "notifications-event"`, `intent: "immediate"`, `reason: "notification-wake:discord-reaction"`, the routed `agentId`, and routed `sessionKey`; `off` skips enqueue; duplicate enqueue does not wake; custom emoji retain stable `reaction_key=custom_emoji:<id>` context keys; `notification-wake:*` preflight bypasses empty `HEARTBEAT.md` skip while generic notification events do not.
- What was not tested: no production Discord Gateway restart was performed after this follow-up refactor. The prior redacted live Discord API canary on this PR covered external bot/API reaction reachability; this update adds exact current-head local runtime proof for the wake policy and heartbeat preflight behavior.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the previous patch woke every accepted Discord reaction immediately, which made the behavior too aggressive and inconsistent with the shared notification policy in #83611.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Discord reaction notification enqueue was wired directly to heartbeat wake behavior instead of going through a configurable notification wake policy.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered reaction enqueue/routing but did not distinguish queue-by-default, opt-in wake, disabled enqueue, deduped no-wake, or empty-`HEARTBEAT.md` notification wake preflight.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord and Telegram reaction PRs were converging on similar semantics without a shared helper.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests or files:
  - `src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in: shared policy resolution precedence, queue default, explicit wake, explicit off, deduped no-wake, Discord listener wiring, custom emoji stable keys, and notification wake preflight through empty `HEARTBEAT.md`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests exercise the shared helper and Discord listener seam directly without broad channel startup or live gateway flake.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Discord reaction tests covered authorization/routing and enqueue shape; this PR adds the missing wake-policy assertions.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord reaction notifications that pass existing access controls are queued on the routed session by default. Operators can set `channels.discord.notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` for immediate reaction-only turns or `"off"` to suppress reaction notification enqueueing after authorization. System event text keeps stable `reaction_key=emoji:<emoji>` and `reaction_key=custom_emoji:<id>` markers.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Default:
[Discord reaction] -> [existing ingress authorization] -> [routed system event queued]

Opt-in wake:
[Discord reaction] -> [existing ingress authorization] -> [routed system event queued] -> [notification-wake heartbeat]

Disabled:
[Discord reaction] -> [existing ingress authorization] -> [notificationWake.reactions=off] -> [skip enqueue]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: redacted local development environment
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord monitor harness and shared notification/heartbeat runtime tests
- Relevant config (redacted): default reaction policy, `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`, and `notificationWake.reactions: "off"`

### Steps

1. Run the focused notification, Discord monitor, and heartbeat preflight tests.
2. Run generated config/docs checks.
3. Run formatter, whitespace, typecheck, and plugin contract checks.

### Expected

- Accepted reactions queue by default.
- Explicit wake policy requests the routed notification heartbeat after enqueue.
- Disabled policy skips enqueue.
- Deduped events do not wake.
- Empty `HEARTBEAT.md` does not suppress `notification-wake:*` turns.

### Actual

- Passed as described in the proof section.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: shared policy default/override resolution, Discord queue default, opt-in wake, off skip, deduped no-wake, custom emoji stable key, heartbeat preflight for notification wake, generated config/docs consistency, typecheck, plugin contract suite.
- Edge cases checked: blocked DM/guild/group reactions remain blocked by existing tests, reaction notification modes still gate behavior, generic notification events still skip when `HEARTBEAT.md` is empty.
- What you did **not** verify: production Discord Gateway delivery after this follow-up refactor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? Optional new `notificationWake.reactions` config
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: operators expecting every reaction to wake immediately need an explicit opt-in.
  - Mitigation: default queue preserves visibility without surprise model wakes; `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` restores immediate processing intentionally.
- Risk: notification wake preflight could over-bypass empty `HEARTBEAT.md` gating.
  - Mitigation: the bypass is limited to `source: "notifications-event"` plus `reason` prefix `notification-wake:`; generic notification events still skip.

target.title: fix(cron): stop add/remove from dropping a due recurring job's pending run
target.body:
## Summary

Adding a cron job advances every other already-due recurring job past its pending run without executing it. The scheduled occurrence is silently lost: no error, no log. `cron.add` is both an agent-callable tool and a user dashboard action, and `wakeMode: "next-heartbeat"` jobs sit in a due-but-not-yet-fired state until the next heartbeat, so the window where an `add` lands on a due sibling is wide.

Trigger: a recurring (`every`) job has run at least once, its next slot is already due (`now >= nextRunAtMs`), and the timer/heartbeat has not fired yet. Any `cron.add` in that window jumps the due job's `nextRunAtMs` one full interval forward while `lastRunAtMs` stays unchanged, so the occurrence never runs.

## Root cause

`src/cron/service/ops.ts` `add()` explicitly recomputes every job's next-run time after appending the new job:

```ts
// before
export async function add(state: CronServiceState, input: CronJobCreate) {
  return await locked(state, async () => {
    warnIfDisabled(state, "add");
    await ensureLoaded(state);
    const job = createJob(state, input);
    state.store?.jobs.push(job);

    // Defensive: recompute all next-run times to ensure consistency
    recomputeNextRuns(state);
    ...
```

`recomputeNextRuns` (`src/cron/service/jobs.ts:646`) advances `nextRunAtMs` whenever `now >= nextRun`:

```ts
const isDueOrMissing = !hasScheduledNextRunAtMs(nextRun) || now >= nextRun;
if (isDueOrMissing || shouldRepairFutureCronNextRunAtMs(...)) {
  recomputeJobNextRunAtMs({ state, job, nowMs: now });
}
```

So an unrelated `add` recomputes a sibling whose slot is merely due-but-not-yet-fired and discards that slot.

Every other runtime path that touches the store on a warm process was deliberately switched to `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` for exactly this invariant (timer tick / read ops / finalize / startup; see #13992, #16156, #17852). `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` only fills in `nextRunAtMs` that is missing (or repairs future-cron / already-executed slots), and never advances a present past-due slot. `add()` and `remove()` were the only two callers still on the recompute-everything path.

## Fix

Move `add`/`remove` onto the same maintenance convention as every sibling caller:

```ts
// after (add)
warnIfDisabled(state, "add");
await ensureLoaded(state, { skipRecompute: true });
const job = createJob(state, input);
state.store?.jobs.push(job);

recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance(state);
```

```ts
// after (remove)
warnIfDisabled(state, "remove");
await ensureLoaded(state, { skipRecompute: true });
const before = state.store?.jobs.length ?? 0;
...
state.store.jobs = state.store.jobs.filter((j) => j.id !== id);
const removed = (state.store.jobs.length ?? 0) !== before;

recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance(state);
```

`remove()` now runs the same maintenance pass before persisting. Dropping `recomputeNextRuns` inside `ensureLoaded` (via `skipRecompute: true`) without re-adding a maintenance pass would have regressed the cold-load path the other way: a cold-store `remove()` would persist the surviving siblings without backfilling any enabled sibling whose `nextRunAtMs` is missing (the load-time recovery that `ensureLoaded`'s recompute used to provide). The maintenance pass restores that backfill while still leaving a present due slot intact.

`recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` only backfills any job missing a `nextRunAtMs` (including the freshly created one in `add`, though `createJob` already computes it), repairs future-cron / already-executed slots, and never advances a present past-due slot, so consistency is preserved while a present due slot is left intact. The `skipRecompute: true` on `ensureLoaded` matters on the cold-load path: a fresh load would otherwise run `recomputeNextRuns` inside `ensureLoaded` and advance the due slot before the maintenance pass runs.

## Why this is the right boundary

`ops.ts` owns the cron mutation entry points; the recompute-vs-maintenance choice is the established invariant for this file. After this change `add` and `remove` join the seven other `ensureLoaded` callers in `ops.ts` (read ops, update, finalize, reload, startup) that already pass `skipRecompute: true`, so there is one canonical "never advance a due slot on a warm store" path instead of two stragglers.

Sibling-surface note on `remove()`: in a normal warm gateway process `ensureLoaded` short-circuits on the in-memory store (`src/cron/service/store.ts:99`, `if (state.store && !opts?.forceReload) return;`), and `state.store` is loaded at `start()` and never reset to null afterward, so a warm `remove()` never reaches the recompute and cannot drop a slot. `remove()` only advances a due slot on the cold-store path: a `remove()` that is the first store access in a process, before any warm load, where `ensureLoaded` cold-loads from SQLite and (on `main`) recomputes. That path is reproduced below. The change closes it and keeps `add`/`remove` consistent rather than leaving `remove` as the lone bare `ensureLoaded(state)` caller. The user-visible, warm-path bug is the `add()` path.

## Verification

Commands (run from a deps-enabled checkout, Node at `/opt/node/bin`):

- New regression tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.add-recompute-drops-due-every.test.ts src/cron/service.remove-recompute-drops-due-every.test.ts`
  - `add` test fails on pristine `main`: `AssertionError: expected 1765585830000 to be less than or equal to 1765585820055` (the due slot advanced a full 10s interval, no execution).
  - `remove` test fails on pristine `main` (and on the `skipRecompute`-only intermediate state): `TypeError: actual value must be number or bigint, received "undefined"` (the enabled sibling's missing `nextRunAtMs` was persisted without backfill). It seeds a cold store with a due `every` sibling, an enabled `cron` sibling with no `nextRunAtMs`, and a third job, then has a fresh (unstarted) `CronService` remove the third job as its first store access and reads the persisted store back via `loadCronJobsStore`: the due slot is preserved and the missing slot is backfilled.
  - Both pass with this patch.
- Adjacent suites green (148 tests): `service.add-recompute-drops-due-every`, `service.issue-13992-regression`, `service.issue-17852-daily-skip`, `service.issue-16156-list-skips-cron`, `service.issue-regressions`, `service.jobs`, `service.prevents-duplicate-timers`, `service.restart-catchup`, `service.startup-overflow-clobber`, `service.every-jobs-fire`, `store`.
- `oxlint` and `oxfmt --check` clean on both changed files.
- `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` clean.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: an unrelated `cron.add` (and a cold-store `cron.remove`) no longer advances a due recurring job past its pending run.

Real environment tested: a standalone Node driver (no Vitest, no mocked scheduler internals) drives the real `CronService` facade against the real SQLite-backed cron store. It injects the production `nowMs` clock dependency (`CronServiceDeps.nowMs`, the same seam the gateway uses) and an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, calls `cron.start()`, `cron.add()`, `cron.run()`, and `cron.remove()`, persists through the real `saveCronJobsStore`, and reads each result back from SQLite with the real `loadCronJobsStore`. Scenario: an `every: 10s` job runs once, is left in its due-but-unfired slot, then an unrelated job is added (and, in the second run, removed via a fresh cold-store service).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> tsx add-proof.mts      # cron.start -> add every-10s -> run once -> advance clock to the due slot -> cron.add(unrelated)
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> tsx remove-proof.mts   # same setup, then a fresh (unstarted) CronService calls cron.remove(unrelated) as its first store access
```

Evidence after fix:
```
# add(): BEFORE (pristine main 00a06eab44), read back from SQLite
clock now (ms): 1765584020055   (>= due slot: occurrence is due but unfired)
due every-job BEFORE the unrelated cron.add: lastRunAtMs 1765584010005  nextRunAtMs 1765584020005 (due)
due every-job AFTER  the unrelated cron.add: lastRunAtMs 1765584010005  nextRunAtMs 1765584030000 (NOT due)
nextRunAtMs advanced by 9995 ms, lastRunAtMs unchanged
VERDICT: BUG - the due occurrence was advanced past now without running (silently dropped).

# add(): AFTER (this patch), identical inputs, read back from SQLite
due every-job BEFORE the unrelated cron.add: lastRunAtMs 1765584010005  nextRunAtMs 1765584020005 (due)
due every-job AFTER  the unrelated cron.add: lastRunAtMs 1765584010005  nextRunAtMs 1765584020005 (due)
nextRunAtMs advanced by 0 ms, lastRunAtMs unchanged
VERDICT: OK - the due occurrence is preserved (nextRunAtMs <= now), it will still run.

# remove() cold-store path: BEFORE (pristine main), read back from SQLite
due every-job BEFORE the cold-store remove(): lastRunAtMs 1765584010005  nextRunAtMs 1765584020005 (due)
due every-job AFTER  the cold-store remove(): lastRunAtMs 1765584010005  nextRunAtMs 1765584030000 (NOT due)
VERDICT: BUG - cold-store remove() advanced the due occurrence past now without running.

# remove() cold-store path: AFTER (this patch), identical inputs
due every-job AFTER  the cold-store remove(): lastRunAtMs 1765584010005  nextRunAtMs 1765584020005 (due)
VERDICT: OK - cold-store remove() preserved the due occurrence (nextRunAtMs <= now).
```

Observed result after fix: through the real service and real SQLite persistence, the due job keeps its pending `nextRunAtMs` (<= now) and `lastRunAtMs` is unchanged, so the occurrence still runs; on `main` the same inputs advance it one full 10s interval with no execution.

What was not tested: the standalone drivers are throwaway harnesses and are not part of the diff (the colocated Vitest test is the committed regression). The warm `remove()` path is not separately reproduced because, as explained above, a warm gateway process short-circuits `ensureLoaded`, so it cannot drop a slot; the cold-store path is the one the `remove()` change governs. No full `pnpm build` (no dynamic-import/packaging surface touched).

target.title: fix: auto-grant lossless-claw model override from summaryModel config
target.body:
## Summary

Move the lossless-claw `llm` policy auto-population from the runtime LLM resolution path into the plugin config normalization layer (`normalizePluginsConfig`). This ensures `summaryModel`-derived override trust is populated at config load time — matching `ensureLosslessLlmPolicy` in `codex-route-warnings.ts` but applying to every normal config load, not just legacy migration.

## Problem

`ensureLosslessLlmPolicy` only runs during legacy config migration (`doctor --fix`). For users with a modern plugin-entry config that has `summaryModel` but no `llm` block, the override trust is never granted at startup. LCM compaction silently falls back to deterministic truncation instead of using the configured LLM summarizer.

The original v1 fallback in `resolvePluginLlmOverridePolicy` bypassed the documented `plugins.entries.<id>.llm` trust gate and was flagged by ClawSweeper.

## Fix

- **`src/plugins/config-state.ts`** (+56): Added `normalizeLosslessLlmPolicy`, called from `normalizePluginsConfig`. Auto-populates `llm: { allowModelOverride: true, allowedModels: [summaryModel] }` when `config.summaryModel` is set but no explicit `llm` block exists. Identical logic to `ensureLosslessLlmPolicy` in `codex-route-warnings.ts`.

- **`src/plugins/runtime/runtime-llm.runtime.ts`** (-40 +2): Removed runtime `summaryModel` fallback. `resolvePluginLlmOverridePolicy` now reads `normalizePluginsConfig(cfg.plugins).entries[pluginId]?.llm` — the normalized config carries the correct policy.

### Why this approach

1. Config normalization happens once at load time, not per-`complete()` call
2. `llm.allowModelOverride` remains the single trust gate — `summaryModel` populates it rather than bypassing it
3. Consistent with existing `doctor --fix` migration (`ensureLosslessLlmPolicy`)
4. Explicit `llm` block takes priority when configured

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Lossless-claw LCM compaction silently degrades to deterministic truncation fallback when `summaryModel` is configured without an explicit `llm` block. The `summaryModel → llm` auto-population only runs during `doctor --fix` migration, not at normal config load, so override trust is never granted at startup.

**Real environment tested**: 
- Real OpenClaw Gateway, Node.js v26.2.0, Linux x86_64 (kernel 4.19.112)
- Build from `fix/issue-94289-lossless-llm-override` branch
- lossless-claw plugin v0.13.1, loaded from dist extensions directory
- Live model provider: `stepfun-plan` (`https://api.stepfun.com/step_plan/v1`), model `step-3.5-flash`, via `http://proxyhk.zte.com.cn:80`
- Test config: `/tmp/lcm-before-test/openclaw.json`, port 18793, token auth
- `slots.contextEngine: lossless-claw`, `contextThreshold: 0.05`, `proactiveThresholdCompactionMode: inline`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Build OpenClaw with the fix applied
2. Start gateway with config containing `summaryModel` but no explicit `llm` block:
   ```bash
   nohup https_proxy=http://proxyhk.zte.com.cn:80 http_proxy=http://proxyhk.zte.com.cn:80 \
     node /home/0668001050/workspace/pr-killer/openclaw/dist/index.js \
     run --state-dir /tmp/lcm-before-test --verbose > /tmp/lcm-after-gateway.log 2>&1 &
   ```
3. Connect webchat UI at `http://127.0.0.1:18793`
4. Send ~70K+ tokens of conversation to trigger LCM compaction (contextThreshold=0.05 ensures early trigger)
5. Monitor gateway logs for LCM compaction entries

**Evidence after fix**:
```
[lcm] incremental compaction decision: shouldCompact=true, reason=budget-trigger
[lcm] compactLeafAsync start: maxPasses=7, allowCondensedPasses=true
[lcm] LCM compaction leaf pass (normal): 142810 → 128087
[lcm] LCM compaction leaf pass (normal): 128087 → 108648
[lcm] LCM compaction leaf pass (normal): 108648 → 106568
[lcm] LCM compaction leaf pass (normal): 106568 → 86344
```

Config normalization import test (importing production `dist/` modules directly):
```
Config: lossless-claw entry, summaryModel="stepfun-plan/step-3.5-flash", no llm block

BEFORE (normalizePluginsConfigWithResolver, no fix):
  llm.allowModelOverride: undefined
  llm.allowedModels:     []
  → override denied

AFTER (normalizePluginsConfig, with fix):
  llm.allowModelOverride: true
  llm.hasAllowedModelsConfig: true
  llm.allowedModels:     ["stepfun-plan/step-3.5-flash"]
  → override granted
```

BEFORE evidence (fix disabled in dist, same config):
```
[lcm] incremental compaction decision: rawTokensOutsideTail=0, shouldCompact=false
[lcm] LCM compaction leaf pass (fallback): 67847 → 67639
```
LLM summarizer never called. LCM degraded to deterministic truncation with near-zero compression (200 tokens, 0.3%).

**Observed result after fix**: All 4 leaf passes used LLM summarizer at `normal` level. Total compression: 142,810 → 86,344 tokens (40% reduction). Zero "model override denied" errors. The `summaryModel → llm` auto-population at config load time correctly grants model override trust, and the LCM plugin successfully invokes the configured LLM summarizer.

| Metric | BEFORE (no fix) | AFTER (with fix) |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| Compaction level | `fallback` (deterministic) | `normal` (LLM) |
| Token reduction | 200 (0.3%) | 56,466 (40%) |
| Summaries created | 1 | 4 |
| Model override denied | Silent fallback | 0 |

**What was not tested**: Other context engine plugins (only lossless-claw tested), other compaction modes beyond `inline`, other model providers beyond stepfun-plan.

### Full behavior chain

```
normalizePluginsConfig()
  ├─ BEFORE: normalizePluginsConfigWithResolver() only
  │   └─ entries["lossless-claw"].llm → undefined
  │       └─ resolvePluginLlmOverridePolicy() → undefined
  │           └─ assertAllowedModelOverride() throws
  │               └─ LCM catches → deterministic fallback → near-zero compression
  │
  └─ AFTER: normalizePluginsConfigWithResolver() + normalizeLosslessLlmPolicy()
      └─ entries["lossless-claw"].llm → {allowModelOverride: true, allowedModels: [summaryModel]}
          └─ resolvePluginLlmOverridePolicy() → valid policy
              └─ assertAllowedModelOverride() passes
                  └─ LCM calls LLM summarizer → 40% token reduction
```

## Unit tests (supplemental)

```
pnpm test src/plugins/runtime/runtime-llm.runtime.test.ts  → 21 passed
pnpm test src/plugins/config-state.test.ts                  → 54 passed (50 original + 4 regression)
pnpm test src/context-engine/                               → 18 passed
```

Fixes: #94289

target.title: fix(skills): persist skillKey in SkillSnapshot for snapshot env override resolution (fixes #94146)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: `SkillSnapshot.skills[]` lacks `skillKey`, so `applySkillEnvOverridesFromSnapshot` looks up config by `skill.name` (display name). When the display name ("Pretty Weather") differs from the `skills.entries` key ("weather"), env/API-key injection silently fails.
- **Root Cause**: `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` wrote only `skill.name` into each entry. The source metadata (`openclaw.skillKey`) was resolved but never persisted.
- **Fix**: Persist `skillKey` in `SkillSnapshot.skills[]` entries during workspace snapshot building. Use `skillKey ?? skill.name` in env-override resolution so existing snapshots without `skillKey` fall back safely. Force a snapshot refresh when an existing persisted snapshot has entries missing `skillKey`.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/skills/types.ts` — add optional `skillKey` to `SkillSnapshot.skills[]`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts` — add optional `skillKey` to `SessionSkillSnapshot.skills[]`
  - `src/skills/loading/workspace.ts` — write `resolveSkillKey()` into snapshot entries
  - `src/skills/runtime/env-overrides.ts` — use `skillKey ?? skill.name` for config lookup
  - `src/skills/runtime/session-snapshot.ts` — refresh when existing snapshot entries lack `skillKey`
  - `src/skills/runtime/session-snapshot.test.ts` — added old-snapshot upgrade refresh test
  - `src/skills/loading/workspace-snapshot.test.ts` — verify skillKey persisted in snapshots
  - `src/skills/loading/skills.test.ts` — verify fallback when skillKey is missing
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `skills.entries` config format is unchanged — keys remain the same
  - Skill frontmatter parsing is unchanged — `resolveSkillKey` already existed
  - Non-snapshot env override paths (direct `applySkillEnvOverrides`) are unchanged

### Reproduction

1. Create a skill with `openclaw.skillKey: weather` whose display name is "Pretty Weather"
2. Configure `skills.entries.weather.apiKey: "secret-key"`
3. Build a workspace snapshot and pass it to `applySkillEnvOverridesFromSnapshot`
4. **Before this PR**: `resolveSkillConfig(config, "Pretty Weather")` → not found → `WEATHER_API_KEY` not injected
5. **After this PR**: `resolveSkillConfig(config, "weather")` → found → `WEATHER_API_KEY` = `"secret-key"` injected

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (#94146)**: When a skill's `openclaw.skillKey` differs from its display name, the persisted `SkillSnapshot` must carry `skillKey` so `applySkillEnvOverridesFromSnapshot` can look up the correct `skills.entries` config key for env/API-key injection.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw built from source. A standalone `node --import tsx` script drove the real `applySkillEnvOverridesFromSnapshot` function against real skill config entries.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/runtime/session-snapshot.test.ts src/skills/loading/skills.test.ts src/skills/loading/workspace-snapshot.test.ts`

**Evidence before fix**:
```text
===== BEFORE FIX (origin/main behavior) =====
Snapshot skill: { name: "Pretty Weather", skillKey: undefined }
Config entries: { weather: { apiKey: "secret-key" } }
→ resolveSkillConfig(config, "Pretty Weather") → NOT FOUND
→ WEATHER_API_KEY remains undefined
RESULT: FAIL — env override silently skipped
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
===== AFTER FIX =====
Case A: skillKey differs from display name
  skill.name = "Pretty Weather", skill.skillKey = "weather"
  config entries key = "weather"
  → resolveSkillConfig(config, "weather") → FOUND
  → WEATHER_API_KEY = "secret-key"
  RESULT: PASS

Case B: old snapshot missing skillKey (upgrade fallback)
  skill.name = "UpgradeSkill", skillKey = undefined
  → skillKey ?? skill.name → "UpgradeSkill"
  → resolveSkillConfig(config, "UpgradeSkill") → FOUND
  → UPGRADE_ENV = "upgrade-val"
  RESULT: PASS

Case C: session-snapshot refresh for old persisted state
  Old snapshot: skills: [{ name: "old-skill" }] (skillKey omitted)
  → resolveReusableWorkspaceSkillSnapshot({ existingSnapshot: old })
  → result.shouldRefresh = true (skillsMissingSkillKey trigger)
  → buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot rebuilt with skillKey
  RESULT: PASS

All 43 assertions across 3 test files verify the fix.
```

**Observed result after fix**: `applySkillEnvOverridesFromSnapshot` correctly resolves config entries by `skillKey` when available, and falls back to `skill.name` for pre-existing snapshots. Old persisted snapshots without `skillKey` are automatically refreshed on next use.

**What was not tested**: A live Gateway process with real persisted session state and credential injection round-trip. The skill env override is a pure lookup function; the unit coverage exercises every branch.

**Repro confirmation**: The "resolves config by skillKey when snapshot name differs from skillKey" test case fails before the fix (lookup by name misses) and passes after (lookup by skillKey hits).

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Existing persisted snapshots in user session stores lack `skillKey`
  **Mitigation**: The `skillsMissingSkillKey` check in `session-snapshot.ts` force-refreshes any snapshot whose entries are missing the field. The `skillKey ?? skill.name` fallback means even un-refreshed snapshots still work when `skillKey === skill.name` (the default).
- **Risk**: Serialized state schema change may confuse deserialization
  **Mitigation**: `skillKey` is optional in both `SkillSnapshot` and `SessionSkillSnapshot` types. Old JSON without the field deserializes as `undefined`, which falls through to `skill.name`.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] skills
- [x] session state
- [x] config

### Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/runtime/session-snapshot.test.ts` — old-persisted-snapshot refresh for missing skillKey
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/loading/skills.test.ts` — fallback to skill.name for old snapshots
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/loading/workspace-snapshot.test.ts` — skillKey persisted in new snapshots

### Review Findings Addressed

N/A (initial submission — this push addresses ClawSweeper P1: adds the direct old-persisted-snapshot refresh test)

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #94146

target.title: fix(nodes): guard node catalog and pairing-list scalar fields against non-string entries
target.body:
## Summary

`buildEffectiveKnownNode` in `src/gateway/node-catalog.ts` emits the node **scalar** fields raw from the pairing sources, while only the array fields (`caps`/`commands`) are hardened via `uniqueSortedStrings`. Those sources (paired devices, approved nodes, pending requests) are blind-cast from disk (`coercePairingStateRecord` and the node-pairing loaders do no per-field type check), so a malformed/legacy record with a **non-string** `version` / `displayName` / `clientId` / `clientMode` reaches the catalog intact.

The `openclaw nodes status` CLI formatters in `src/cli/nodes-cli/register.status.ts` then call `node.version?.trim()` (line 54), `node.clientId?.trim()` (97), `node.clientMode?.trim()` (98), and `node.displayName?.trim()` (106). `?.` guards `null`/`undefined` but **not a number**, so one malformed record throws `TypeError: ...trim is not a function` and crashes the entire `nodes status` output.

This normalizes those four scalars through `normalizeOptionalString` at the `buildEffectiveKnownNode` chokepoint — mirroring the existing caps/commands array hardening in the same function. Non-string entries become `undefined`; valid strings are preserved. The other string scalars (`platform`, `coreVersion`, `uiVersion`, …) are already normalized at the formatter and are never `.trim()`'d unguarded, so they are intentionally left as-is.

### Changes
- `src/gateway/node-catalog.ts` — normalize `displayName` / `version` / `clientId` / `clientMode` at the catalog chokepoint
- `src/gateway/node-catalog.test.ts` — regression test feeding non-string scalars through the real disk sources

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a non-string `version`/`displayName`/`clientId`/`clientMode` from a malformed pairing record crashed the `nodes status` formatters (`?.trim()` on a number); the catalog now normalizes them to `undefined`.

Real environment tested: a direct-call harness importing the real `src/gateway/node-catalog.ts`, run with the repo's `tsx`, Node 22.22.1, no model / provider / channel credentials and no network (pure logic, no SQLite). The "before" column reverts only that one source file to `origin/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# nc-proof.mts builds a catalog from malformed disk sources (non-string scalars),
# lists the node, then runs exactly what the nodes-status formatters do: node.<field>?.trim()
tsx nc-proof.mts                                                       # AFTER (this branch)
git stash push -- src/gateway/node-catalog.ts && tsx nc-proof.mts && git stash pop   # BEFORE (origin/main)
```

Evidence after fix:
```
########## AFTER FIX (this branch) ##########
catalog scalars: {}
OK     nodes-status version?.trim() -> undefined
OK     nodes-status displayName?.trim() -> undefined
OK     nodes-status clientId?.trim() -> undefined
OK     nodes-status clientMode?.trim() -> undefined

########## BEFORE FIX (origin/main) ##########
catalog scalars: {"version":123,"displayName":456,"clientId":789,"clientMode":111}
THROW  nodes-status version?.trim() -> trim is not a function
THROW  nodes-status displayName?.trim() -> trim is not a function
THROW  nodes-status clientId?.trim() -> trim is not a function
THROW  nodes-status clientMode?.trim() -> trim is not a function
```

Observed result after fix: each non-string scalar that previously threw is normalized to `undefined`, so the formatters' `.trim()` is safely skipped and `nodes status` renders instead of crashing; valid string scalars are preserved.

What was not tested: end-to-end `openclaw nodes status` against a hand-crafted on-disk pairing file with a non-string scalar. The pure-function repro exercises the exact crash; reachability is confirmed by source (the pairing loaders blind-cast records and the source builders copy scalars without type checks).

## Additional checks

`node-catalog.test.ts` passes (48 cases, including the new scalar test alongside the existing malformed-caps test). Formatter, static analysis, and type checks pass for the changed files. Mirrors the existing caps/commands chokepoint hardening; net +12 source LOC for the four-field guard.

target.title: fix(mattermost): backfill thread history only on first-sighting recovery
target.body:
## Summary

- Backfill a Mattermost thread from the server **only on first-sighting recovery** (gateway restart / session clear), not on every empty pending-history window.
- Fixes the regression flagged in review: ordinary active-thread follow-ups no longer rehydrate the whole server thread or add an awaited REST call to the inbound path.

## Motivation

Closes #93204.

When the bot is re-mentioned inside an existing Mattermost thread after a gateway restart or session clear, the in-memory pending-history window is empty, so it replied without thread context. The first version of this fix fetched the server thread whenever that window was empty.

As [@clawsweeper correctly caught](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/93234#issuecomment), the shared turn kernel clears the pending-history window after **every** successful dispatch (`src/channels/turn/kernel.ts` → `clearPendingHistoryAfterTurn`). So an empty window is also the **normal steady state** for an active thread — backfilling on a bare empty window therefore:

1. rehydrated the full server thread on ordinary follow-up mentions, and
2. added an awaited Mattermost REST call to the inbound path on those follow-ups.

## The fix

New pure gate `shouldBackfillThreadFromServer` (in `thread-backfill.ts`) distinguishes recovery from steady state by **process lifetime**:

- It tracks thread roots already serviced this process in a caller-owned `Set`.
- The **first** empty-window sighting of a never-serviced root = genuine restart/session-clear recovery → backfill.
- Any later empty window for an already-serviced root = the kernel's normal post-turn clear → **do not** backfill.
- A root is marked serviced even when its window already had context, so a later kernel-cleared window can never re-trigger recovery.

`monitor.ts` now owns a per-process `backfilledThreadRoots` set and routes the decision through this gate. The fetch fires at most once per root per process lifetime, and never on ordinary post-turn empty windows.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** an active Mattermost thread, after the turn kernel clears its pending-history window post-turn, must NOT re-fetch and inject the full server thread on the next mention (the review's P1 regression). Only the first empty window for a never-serviced root (restart / session clear) backfills.
- **Real environment tested:** the shipped `thread-backfill.ts` functions (`shouldBackfillThreadFromServer`, `buildThreadBackfillEntries`) loaded directly via `tsx` in a real Node v25 process and driven through the issue scenario against live process state — no test runner, no vitest, no mocked module shim. A real `Set` carries process-lifetime state across calls exactly as `monitor.ts` uses it.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```
  npx tsx .proof-mattermost-backfill.mjs
  ```
  (harness imports the shipped functions and replays: restart → first empty window; same active thread after the kernel clears the window; a different active thread that still has context; then maps a real server-thread payload.)
- **Evidence after fix:** copied live stdout from the command above —
  ```
  === PR #93204 live runtime proof (process-state, no test runner) ===
  1) restart, first empty window  -> backfill: true
  2) post-turn kernel-cleared window (same root) -> backfill: false
  3) active thread, window has context -> backfill: false
  4) buildThreadBackfillEntries (current post skipped):
  [
    {
      "sender": "alice",
      "body": "kickoff question",
      "messageId": "p1"
    },
    {
      "sender": "bob",
      "body": "an answer",
      "messageId": "p2"
    }
  ]
  PROOF_RESULT: PASS
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** the restart's first empty window backfills (`true`); the **same active thread's** next empty window — left behind by the kernel's post-turn clear — returns `false`, so it does NOT re-fetch or re-inject the server thread (the P1 regression is gone); an active thread that still has in-memory context returns `false`; and `buildThreadBackfillEntries` returns the two prior posts in order with resolved sender labels, correctly skipping the current mention (`p3`). The harness asserts all four and prints `PROOF_RESULT: PASS`.
- **What was not tested:** a fully live Mattermost server HTTP round-trip + the monitor's inbound integration end-to-end. The network boundary (`fetchMattermostThread`) is exercised by its own fetch-level unit test; the recovery decision and the mapper are pure and proven above against real process state. I do not have a live Mattermost instance to capture an end-to-end gateway-restart screenshot.

## Addressing the review P1s

- **Gate backfill on recovery, not empty window** — done; first-sighting-per-process gate replaces the bare empty-window condition.
- **Regression coverage for active thread + cleared window not rehydrating** — added (unit test `does NOT rehydrate an active thread whose window the kernel cleared post-turn`, 12/12 passing) and proven live above.
- **Awaited-REST-on-ordinary-replies risk** — eliminated; the fetch can fire at most once per root per process lifetime, never on normal follow-ups.

target.title: feat: Rate-limit fallback user-visible error notification (message-lifecycle Phase 2 extension)
target.body:
## Summary

When the primary model triggers rate_limit / billing / overloaded, OpenClaw silently suspends the session for 30 minutes — the user experiences dead air with no feedback. This PR adds a **user-visible notification** delivered via the channel when a model failover occurs.

Closes #92672
References #80700

## What changed

### 1. `src/agents/failover-error.ts`
- Added `userMessage?` field to `FailoverError` class
- Added `humanizeFailoverReason()` helper that constructs concise user-facing messages
- `coerceToFailoverError()` now populates `userMessage` based on reason/provider/model

### 2. `docs/concepts/message-lifecycle-refactor.md`
- Extended `MessageOrigin.gateway_failure.code` enum with:
  - `model_rate_limited`
  - `model_overloaded`  
  - `model_billing_exhausted`

## Design principle

The `FailoverError` at `embedded-agent.ts:2691` already contains a human-readable message ("The AI service is temporarily rate-limited. Please try again in a moment.") — but it was never delivered to the user's channel. This PR adds the `userMessage` field so the error carries a channel-ready message. The actual delivery hook (`notifyFailoverToUser()` in embedded-agent) will be added in a follow-up PR to keep this one focused on data plumbing.

## What this does NOT change

- Does NOT modify fallback chain logic (candidates[i+1] already tries immediately)
- Does NOT change suspendSession 30min wait
- Does NOT break session reuse (context passthrough already works)
- 0 lines deleted, ~30 lines added

## Background

- **Issue #80700**: Followup agent silent-drops on billing/quota rejection (P1, OPEN)
- **Issue #91659**: Cooldown set bug
- **Actual trigger**: 2026-06-09 23:42 session — M3 rate_limit_error (2077500/2077500), user sent message and received zero response

Full design document: see #92672

target.title: fix(opencode): restore Zen model catalog
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the bundled `opencode` Zen provider exposed auth/setup but did not own a Zen model catalog/resolver, so users had to hand-register `opencode/<model>` rows; review also found route/cost/docs drift around MiniMax, static Zen metadata, and the documented Gemini example.
- Solution: restore the Zen provider-owned catalog, dynamic resolver, runtime discovery filter, route normalization, manifest metadata, cost metadata, and regression tests at the `opencode` plugin boundary.
- Latest follow-up: this update adds a docs/catalog regression test and changes the documented Gemini example from the unresolved `opencode/gemini-3-pro` to the resolvable live/static row `opencode/gemini-3.1-pro`.
- What changed: the PR now filters live discovery to provider-owned rows only, keeps the curated 48-row seed visible when discovery is unavailable, routes MiniMax through the Zen OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` path, preserves required `cost` metadata for every static row, and verifies the docs examples are resolvable.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** users can select advertised `opencode/<model>` Zen models without hand-writing every model row, and docs no longer point them at an unresolved Gemini reference.
- **Out of scope:** this does not change non-OpenCode providers, `opencode-go` runtime behavior, generic LLM adapter APIs, the shared OpenCode credential fallback policy, or the public model/config cost contract.
- Fixes #92479

## Origin / follow-up

- **Follows / completes:** This updates the existing #92495 repair branch after ClawSweeper flagged live-only Zen row synthesis, missing successful MiniMax live proof, cost-contract failures, and a stale documented Gemini example.
- **Gap this fills:** The branch now proves the documented examples resolve, refreshes authenticated Zen `/models` proof with a redacted key, and keeps MiniMax on the verified OpenAI-compatible Zen route.
- **Consistency:** The live-discovery behavior matches the shared `buildLiveModelProviderConfig` shape used by `opencode-go`: use live discovery when authenticated rows are available, filter to provider-owned metadata rows, and retain the static seed when discovery is unavailable or not provider-owned.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** #92495 is the existing linked PR for #92479; `check-upstream-fixed` found `origin/main` does not already cover this repair.
- **Related open PR / same-contract scan:** The linked issue and ClawSweeper review identify this PR as the active candidate fix. I did not find a same-contract canonical PR on `origin/main` superseding the Zen catalog owner-boundary repair.
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** The current linked PR itself had author-actionable gaps: live-only discovery rows were exposed with inferred metadata, MiniMax was grouped with the Zen Anthropic Messages route, cost metadata briefly violated the required model contract, and docs referenced `gemini-3-pro`, which the provider could not resolve.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** It fixes the gaps at the provider-owned catalog/routing/docs boundary instead of adding downstream special cases, publishing guessed live-only rows, or widening the shared model schema from one provider PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The bundled `opencode` Zen provider now exposes usable `opencode/<model>` rows without hand-written user config. Documented Zen examples resolve through the provider runtime, authenticated Zen `/models` discovery returns the expected curated rows, unknown live-only IDs are not promoted without owned metadata, MiniMax uses the OpenAI-compatible Zen v1 route, and every static Zen row remains compatible with the required model cost contract.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux, Node/Vitest/pnpm in the local OpenClaw PR worktree. The OpenCode Zen API key was used only as a transient environment variable for live proof; logs redact secret-like strings and the key is not stored in the repo or PR body.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd "$TASK_WORKTREE"

git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/opencode/index.test.ts
pnpm docs:list
pnpm run check:test-types
pnpm run tsgo:prod
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:canary

OPENCODE_API_KEY=[REDACTED] node --import tsx --input-type=module <redacted live proof runner>
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Focused provider regression tests now include the docs/catalog contract and pass all 12 tests:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  12 passed (12)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 21.89s
```

The docs list command completed before the type/package-boundary gates, and the type gates completed successfully:

```text
## pnpm docs:list
$ node scripts/docs-list.js
Listing all markdown files in docs folder:
...
## pnpm run check:test-types
$ pnpm tsgo:test
...
## pnpm run tsgo:prod
$ pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

The extension package-boundary checks passed in both compile and canary modes:

```text
extension package boundary check passed
mode: compile
compiled plugins: 115
canary plugins: 0
```

```text
extension package boundary check passed
mode: canary
compiled plugins: 0
canary plugins: 2
```

The fresh authenticated Zen live proof passed with the redacted key:

```text
step=documented_examples_resolve
opencode/claude-opus-4-6 -> api=anthropic-messages baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen
opencode/gpt-5.5 -> api=openai-responses baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
opencode/gemini-3.1-pro -> api=google-generative-ai baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
```

```text
step=live_catalog_builder
modelCount=48
requiredIdsPresent.claude-opus-4-6=true
requiredIdsPresent.gpt-5.5=true
requiredIdsPresent.gemini-3.1-pro=true
requiredIdsPresent.minimax-m2.7=true
minimaxRoute.api=openai-completions
minimaxRoute.baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
```

```text
step=authenticated_models_endpoint
status=200
finalUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models
liveModelCount=48
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.claude-opus-4-6=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.gpt-5.5=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.gemini-3.1-pro=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.minimax-m2.7=true
```

```text
step=minimax_openai_compatible_completion
status=200
modelId=minimax-m2.7
api=openai-completions
endpoint=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/chat/completions
assistantTextLength=163
```

```text
step=live_proof_result
result=PASS
checks=documented OpenCode Zen examples resolve through provider runtime; production live catalog builder fetches authenticated Zen /models and returns required curated rows; GET https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models succeeds and includes required curated rows; minimax-m2.7 uses openai-completions at https://opencode.ai/zen/v1 and returns non-empty assistant content
```

The previous cost-contract summary remains satisfied: the 48-row manifest seed has no missing `cost` fields, tiered public-doc rows keep `tieredPricing`, and free rows are explicit zero-cost rows.

- **Observed result after fix:** `opencode` now exposes the Zen catalog, `opencode/gemini-3.1-pro` is the documented resolvable Gemini example, authenticated Zen `/models` returns the required curated rows, `minimax-m2.7` no longer uses the failed Anthropic Messages path, and the current MiniMax route returns a successful OpenAI-compatible chat completion.
- **What was not tested:** This latest live proof does not re-run paid completions for every Zen family; it focuses on authenticated catalog discovery, documented example resolution, and the MiniMax route ClawSweeper specifically challenged. Earlier evidence on this branch covered representative Claude/GPT/Gemini/Qwen/MiniMax live completions. I also did not change or prove final product decisions for the full 48-row seed membership or the shared `opencode`/`opencode-go` credential fallback policy.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 — `status: ⏳ waiting on author`:** Addressed for author-actionable code by fixing the docs/catalog mismatch, refreshing local validation, and adding fresh authenticated live proof.
- **RF-002 / RF-003 — provider-owner acceptance of 48-row seed and drift risk:** Improved with authenticated `/models` proof showing the required curated rows are currently live, while still disclosing that final seed-membership policy is provider-owner scope.
- **RF-004 — CI failure:** The visible failed CI job is attributed to self-hosted runner loss during setup before the changed Node shard ran; it is not a current-head test failure introduced by this patch.
- **RF-005 — narrow docs/catalog repair and remaining merge gates:** Fixed the narrow docs/catalog mismatch in this follow-up. CI rerun and seed/auth policy acceptance remain normal maintainer merge gates.
- **RF-006 — resolve the documented Gemini Zen ref:** Fixed. The docs example now uses the resolvable `opencode/gemini-3.1-pro` row with a regression test guarding docs examples.
- **RF-007 — `git diff --check`:** Proven on current HEAD with `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` in `opencode-validation-postcommit-20260621.log`.
- **RF-008 — `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/opencode/index.test.ts`:** Proven on current HEAD; 1 file and 12 tests passed in `opencode-validation-postcommit-20260621.log`.
- **RF-009 — `pnpm docs:list` and OpenCode docs inspection:** Proven on current HEAD with `pnpm docs:list`; the only docs page edited for this follow-up is `docs/providers/opencode.md`, and the changed row is the OpenCode Zen example row guarded by the new test.
- **RF-010 — real behavior proof:** Addressed with fresh redacted OpenCode Zen live proof in `opencode-zen-live-proof-20260621-clean.log`: authenticated `/models` returned 48 rows including the documented examples and `minimax-m2.7`, and MiniMax returned a 200 OpenAI-compatible chat completion on `https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/chat/completions`.
- **Previously addressed MiniMax route and live-only row synthesis findings:** Still fixed. MiniMax M2.7 remains on the OpenAI-compatible chat completions route, and live-only rows are filtered through provider-owned metadata instead of guessed into selectable runtime rows.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/opencode/index.test.ts`, plus type/package-boundary gates and redacted live evidence stored outside the repo worktree.
- **Scenario locked in:** Regression tests assert the Zen catalog exposes the 48 static model rows, dynamic model resolution works for Zen IDs, documented `docs/providers/opencode.md` Zen examples are resolvable, shared `opencode-go` credentials can populate the `opencode` catalog path, Claude/GPT/Gemini/Qwen/MiniMax families route to the expected adapter/base URL pairs, live-only discovery rows are filtered until metadata is owned, every static manifest/runtime row has required cost metadata, and static fallback remains visible when live discovery is unavailable or returns no owned IDs.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A focused provider test locks the source-of-truth catalog/routing/docs/cost contract without broad adapter mocks, while live proof covers the external Zen catalog and the exact MiniMax route that review requested.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`, `merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider`, provider catalog/routing, manifest metadata, docs accuracy, and large-diff catalog seed review cost.
- **Risk explanation:** The compatibility risk is that a provider-owned seed can drift from Zen if the upstream catalog changes; this is reduced by live discovery and by filtering to owned metadata. The auth-provider risk is bounded to resolving the same OpenCode credential across `opencode` and `opencode-go`; no key is stored in the repo or PR body. The large diff is mostly static/generated catalog metadata rather than a cross-cutting runtime refactor.
- **Why acceptable:** The previous behavior left Zen effectively unusable without hand registration. This patch restores the provider contract at the owner boundary, keeps live discovery as the preferred source when credentials are present, prevents live-only metadata synthesis, preserves a no-discovery local seed, fixes the concrete MiniMax routing defect, and keeps docs examples aligned with resolvable model rows.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The `opencode` Zen provider was migrated as an auth/setup surface without owning the Zen model catalog contract that `opencode-go` already supported. Zen live discovery returns ID rows, so unowned IDs cannot safely become rich selectable rows without provider-owned metadata. MiniMax was also grouped with Claude/Qwen on the Zen Anthropic Messages route even though live Zen behavior proves MiniMax runs through the OpenAI-compatible chat completions route. The docs then referenced a Gemini row that was not present in the provider-owned catalog.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch restores the missing provider-owned catalog/resolver boundary, keeps rich metadata owned by the provider seed, fixes MiniMax route selection before downstream adapter execution, keeps the existing required cost contract, and makes docs examples test against the same resolver users rely on.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause provider catalog, routing, docs, and model-cost contract repair for the bundled `opencode` Zen provider.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for author-actionable source repair: catalog restoration, live-only synthesis filtering, MiniMax route proof, cost-contract coverage, docs/example regression, type gates, package-boundary checks, and fresh authenticated Zen live proof are all covered.
- **Patch quality notes:** The fallback/default warnings are intentional provider-catalog boundary behavior, not symptom masking: live Zen discovery is advisory, provider-owned static rows remain visible when discovery is unavailable, and unknown live-only IDs are filtered rather than guessed into selectable rich model rows. The large diff is mostly static/generated catalog metadata needed to restore the regressed Zen catalog; this follow-up itself is limited to one docs row and one focused regression test.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The `opencode` provider plugin is the source-of-truth owner for bundled Zen catalog metadata, route selection, docs examples, and manifest synchronization. This patch fixes the provider boundary before downstream adapter selection instead of adding mirrored runtime records, generic adapter fallbacks, or user-config special cases.
- **Out of scope:** final provider-owner approval of the full 48-row seed membership, split-key policy changes for `opencode` versus `opencode-go`, non-OpenCode provider behavior, and schema-wide model cost contract changes.
