target.title: fix(slack): forward per-agent identity overlay on heartbeat and runtimeSend (#84297)
target.body: ## Summary Per-agent Slack identity (`agents.list[].identity`) doesn't survive the heartbeat / announce path. Two things go wrong: 1. The overlay gets dropped before it reaches the channel adapter — both in the heartbeat runner and in the legacy CLI `runtimeSend` factory. 2. Even when identity *does* reach the Slack adapter, the helper that maps `OutboundIdentity.emoji` → `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji` only accepts `:shortcode:` form and silently throws away raw Unicode emoji like `📟` — which is exactly how most people configure it. So every heartbeat-driven message posts under the generic app bot instead of the configured persona ("Pulse 📟"). The reply path already handles this correctly (since #38235); the announce/heartbeat path was just never wired up. **The fix:** thread identity through the heartbeat runner and the `runtimeSend` factory to the adapter, and stop the Slack adapter from filtering raw Unicode emoji so the configured value actually reaches `icon_emoji`. **What changed:** - Added `RuntimeSendOpts.identity?: OutboundIdentity`, forwarded to every adapter entrypoint (`sendText` / `sendMedia` / `sendPayload`). - The heartbeat runner resolves the agent's identity once (`resolveAgentOutboundIdentity(cfg, agentId)`, right next to `outboundSession`) and passes it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` in `extensions/slack/src/outbound-adapter.ts` now passes `identity.emoji` through untouched — the same thing the reply path already does in `src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts`. - Tests: unit tests for the `runtimeSend` factory, an end-to-end test that drives `runHeartbeatOnce` against a real Slack test plugin, and 4 adapter tests (raw Unicode, `:shortcode:`, `iconUrl`-wins, no-identity). **Proof status:** the live-Slack proof ClawSweeper asked for (`status: 📣 needs proof`) is now in hand — see **Real behavior proof** below. The reporter (@aw-stevens), who runs the affected `chat:write.customize` deployment, confirmed on the real workspace that Slack accepts the raw `📟` (`ok:true`, no fallback) and that the `username` override flips the message off the shared app identity onto the per-agent persona. **What this PR deliberately leaves alone:** the cron `--announce` path. On current `main` it already goes through `resolveAgentOutboundIdentity` (`src/cron/delivery.ts`, lines 68 / 105). The reporter's original trace was against the v2026.5.18 dist, where both cron and heartbeat still ran through the legacy factory; cron has since moved to `sendDurableMessageBatch` with identity wired up, but heartbeat kept the bug. The reply path (#38235), the missing-scope retry, and `icon_url`-wins-over-`icon_emoji` are all untouched. ## Motivation In a shared Slack channel, the per-agent identity overlay is the only thing that tells you *which* agent posted what. Without it, every cron digest and every heartbeat push from every agent shows up under one generic app bot name and icon — and operators are forced into ugly workarounds: prefixing every template with the agent name by hand, or giving each agent its own channel and losing the whole point of a shared channel. The reporter laid this out cleanly in #84297 with a source trace pointing straight at the gap in the runtime-send factory. ClawSweeper's review then caught a second, related problem: the Slack adapter's raw-Unicode emoji filter, which would have left the persona half-broken even after the threading fix. The reporter has since gone and captured the live `chat:write.customize` proof for both Slack-side unknowns (see **Real behavior proof**). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #84297 - Related #38235 (reply-path identity fix this PR complements) - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) Proven on both sides of the seam: OC threads `agents.list[].identity` into `chat.postMessage` (source-level plus the new tests), and Slack itself accepts and renders that exact call shape — the latter captured live by the reporter (@aw-stevens) on the real `chat:write.customize` workspace #84297 was filed from. The OC half is what our code controls; the Slack half is server behavior no source-level test can reach. Together they close the `status: 📣 needs proof` gate. - Behavior or issue addressed: per-agent Slack identity (`agents.list[].identity` → `name` / `emoji` / `avatarUrl`) is applied on heartbeat target-channel pushes and through the legacy CLI `runtimeSend` factory, and the raw Unicode emoji form (`📟`) survives end-to-end onto `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji`. - Real environment tested: two complementary setups. (1) OC side — from this machine (no live Slack bot here) I ran the patched runtime semantics directly under `node`: `createChannelOutboundRuntimeSend.buildContext()`, the heartbeat-runner identity wiring, and the Slack adapter's `resolveSlackSendIdentity`. (2) Slack side — the reporter ran a controlled `chat.postMessage` pair from the real shared bot token on the production `chat:write.customize` workspace, reproducing the exact call the patched adapter emits. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the verification script locally, then the reporter ran a live control/customized pair on the real workspace. ``` node verify-identity-fix.mjs ``` On the reporter's live workspace: a control `chat.postMessage` with no overrides, then a customized one (`username` = Pulse, `icon_emoji` = raw Unicode 📟) from the same bot token seconds apart into the same channel, followed by a `conversations.history` read-back. - Evidence after fix (terminal capture plus live Slack API responses): real stdout from the local run — ```text Verifying identity propagation through runtime-send + heartbeat-runner + Slack adapter (issue #84297) [1/6] runtime sendText receives identity (name=Pulse, emoji=📟) [2/6] runtime sendMedia receives identity (name=Pulse) [3/6] runtime omits identity when caller does not supply it (identity=undefined) [4/6] heartbeat-runner threads identity into both batches (name=Pulse, emoji=📟) [5/6] Slack adapter preserves raw Unicode emoji on icon_emoji (iconEmoji=📟) ← reviewer P2 [6/6] Slack adapter still accepts :shortcode: form for callers (iconEmoji=:beeper:) All 6 scenarios pass. Per-agent persona overlay reaches chat.postMessage with raw Unicode emoji preserved. ``` Live `chat:write.customize` scope, straight from the response header (no posting required): ``` x-oauth-scopes: chat:write, chat:write.customize, app_mentions:read, … (truncated) ``` `chat.postMessage` responses (post-call echo) — control vs. customized, same bot token: ```jsonc // (1) CONTROL — no override { "ok": true, "ts": "…820.7", "echo": { "bot_id": "B0…JG17S", "bot_profile_name": "DemandBots" // ← generic shared app identity } } // (2) CUSTOMIZED — username + raw-Unicode icon_emoji { "ok": true, "ts": "…821.4", "echo": { "username": "Pulse", // ← per-agent name overrides "DemandBots" "icons": { "emoji": "📟" }, // ← RAW UNICODE accepted & applied, ok:true "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" // ← SAME token as the control } } ``` `conversations.history` read-back (authoritative persisted render): ```jsonc [ { "ts": "…821.4", "subtype": "bot_message", "username": "Pulse", "icons": { "emoji": "📟" }, "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" }, // customized { "ts": "…820.7", "bot_profile": { "name": "DemandBots" }, "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" } // control ] ``` Rendered Slack bubbles — the control posts under the generic "DemandBots" app, the customized one posts under "Pulse 📟": - Observed result after fix: `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` reaches `ChannelOutboundContext.identity` on every send shape; `runHeartbeatOnce` resolves identity once and threads it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls (heartbeat-ok and main reply); `resolveSlackSendIdentity` hands the raw emoji straight to `chat.postMessage`. Live, Slack accepted the raw 📟 (`ok:true`, persisted as `icons.emoji` in `conversations.history`, no fallback and no `:emoji_not_found:`), and the `username` override rendered the per-agent persona ("Pulse") distinct from the shared "DemandBots" identity — same token, seconds apart. That clears the P2 / `merge-risk: message-delivery` concern: Slack does not reject raw Unicode here, so the passthrough needs no normalization. The missing-scope fallback is unchanged, so workspaces without `chat:write.customize` still degrade to the app identity. - What was not tested: a fully OC-generated heartbeat fire (`runHeartbeatOnce` or the scheduler) into a live workspace, as opposed to the controlled `chat.postMessage` that emits the same call shape. The reporter's `auth.test` curl in #84297 confirms scope is granted on the affected deployment, so the OC wiring is the load-bearing change; a maintainer can run the final visual check with `@openclaw-mantis slack desktop smoke: verify an agent heartbeat to Slack with identity name and emoji renders under that persona.` - Before evidence: on unpatched `main` (2026.5.26), the 8:07 AM heartbeat/cron announce lands under the generic "DemandBots" app, while the 4:48 PM @-mention reply renders correctly aliased (the post-#38235 reply path) — so the announce/heartbeat path is the gap. The #84297 write-up (evidence A/B/C) documents the v2026.5.18 behavior: scope granted, `buildContext()` dropping `opts.identity`, and the delivery mirror showing zero `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` references. ## Root Cause Three layered gaps: 1. **The heartbeat runner never forwarded identity.** Both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls (heartbeat-ok around L1701, main reply around L1981) passed `session: outboundSession` and `deps: opts.deps`, but no `identity`. 2. **The `runtimeSend` factory dropped it too.** `buildContext()` in `channel-outbound-send.ts` didn't carry `opts.identity` through on the legacy CLI path. 3. **The Slack adapter filtered out raw Unicode.** Even with identity threaded through, `resolveSlackSendIdentity` ran `emoji` through `/^:[^:\s]+:$/` and turned anything that wasn't `:shortcode:` into `undefined` — so `identity.emoji: "📟"` got stripped before it ever reached `icon_emoji`. Why nothing caught it: there was no test checking that resolved identity reaches the adapter on the announce/heartbeat path, and nothing exercising the adapter with raw Unicode. #38235 wired up the reply path, but only covered the streaming reply context — the heartbeat path builds its own send-batch shape that fix never touched, and the emoji filter was never tested against the raw input people actually use. Context worth knowing: cron `--announce` moved off the legacy factory onto `sendDurableMessageBatch` between v2026.5.18 and `main`, and happened to thread identity correctly on the way. Heartbeat went through the same migration but kept the bug. That's why the reporter's v2026.5.18 trace showed both broken, while on `main` cron is fine and heartbeat isn't — and the emoji filter has been quietly degrading every caller (cron included) that uses a raw-Unicode emoji. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - **Where:** `src/cli/send-runtime/channel-outbound-send.test.ts` (4 new cases), `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.identity.test.ts` (new file, 2 cases driving `runHeartbeatOnce` against a real Slack test plugin), and `extensions/slack/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts` (4 cases: raw Unicode, `:shortcode:`, `iconUrl`-wins, no-identity). - **What they lock in:** when `agents.list[].identity` is set, the resolved `OutboundIdentity` reaches the adapter on every `runtimeSend` shape (`sendText` / `sendMedia` / `sendPayload`); the heartbeat runner threads it into the Slack send dep on both the heartbeat-ok and main reply paths; and `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps the configured emoji verbatim, raw (`📟`) or shortcode (`:beeper:`). With no identity configured, the field stays `undefined`, so nothing changes for existing callers. - **Why this is enough:** the bug lives in exactly three places — the factory's `buildContext()`, the heartbeat runner's two batch calls, and the adapter's emoji mapping — and all three now have focused coverage that needs no live workspace. The integration test runs the real `runHeartbeatOnce` through the existing `heartbeat-runner.test-harness` Slack plugin, so a regression at any layer fails a test. - **Existing coverage:** none for the announce/heartbeat path or the raw-emoji mapping. `send.identity-fallback.test.ts` covers the missing-scope retry, but only with `:shortcode:` form, which never tripped the filter. - If no new test is added: N/A — new tests are added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Heartbeat-driven Slack messages now render under the configured per-agent persona on any workspace that grants `chat:write.customize` — matching what the reply path has done since #38235. `agents.list[].identity.emoji` can now be a raw Unicode emoji (`"📟"`) and it's applied verbatim to `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji`; previously this adapter path only honored `:shortcode:`. Workspaces without `chat:write.customize` keep falling back to the app identity through the existing retry, exactly as before. Other channels with identity-overlay support (Discord webhook routing, Feishu cards) pick up the factory fix for free if they hit that path. No new config and no new flags — this just honors the `agents.list[].identity` you already have, on paths that used to drop or filter it. ## Diagram How the heartbeat path and the adapter behave before vs. after: ```text Before (heartbeat path): [heartbeat tick] -> resolveDeliveryTarget -> outboundSession -> sendDurableMessageBatch({ session, deps }) ^ identity dropped here -> Slack adapter sees ctx.identity = undefined -> chat.postMessage with no username/icon overrides -> message renders under generic app identity Before (Slack adapter, even when identity does arrive): ctx.identity.emoji = "RAW UNICODE" -> resolveSlackSendIdentity() -> /^:[^:\s]+:$/.test(raw unicode) === false -> iconEmoji = undefined (raw Unicode silently dropped) -> chat.postMessage with no icon_emoji -> message renders under generic app identity After (heartbeat path): [heartbeat tick] -> resolveDeliveryTarget -> outboundSession -> resolveAgentOutboundIdentity(cfg, agentId) -> sendDurableMessageBatch({ session, identity, deps }) -> Slack adapter resolveSlackSendIdentity(ctx.identity) -> chat.postMessage with username + icon_emoji -> message renders under "Pulse" After (Slack adapter): ctx.identity.emoji = "RAW UNICODE" (or ":beeper:") -> resolveSlackSendIdentity() -> iconEmoji = ctx.identity.emoji (raw value passed through) -> chat.postMessage with icon_emoji = the configured value -> message renders under the configured persona Legacy runtimeSend factory (channel-outbound-send.ts): Before: buildContext() -> { cfg, to, text, ... gatewayClientScopes } (identity silently dropped) After: buildContext() -> { cfg, to, text, ... gatewayClientScopes, identity } ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No — it's the same `chat.postMessage` call, just with `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` filled in from existing config when the agent has an identity overlay. - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - Channel: Slack via `@openclaw/slack`, socket mode. The bot scope needs `chat:write.customize` for the overlay to take effect server-side. Heartbeat set to `target: "slack"`, `to: "channel:C0XXXXXXX"`. - Config (redacted): ```yaml agents: list: - id: pulse identity: name: Pulse emoji: 📟 heartbeat: every: 2h target: slack to: channel:C0XXXXXXX accountId: default ``` ### Steps 1. Fire a heartbeat tick for an agent with `identity` configured (`runHeartbeatOnce`, or wait for the scheduler) targeting a Slack channel. 2. Watch the message land in the channel. 3. Before this patch it shows up under the generic app bot + default icon. After, it shows up under "Pulse 📟" on any `chat:write.customize` workspace — whether the emoji is raw Unicode or `:shortcode:`. ### Expected The heartbeat message shows the per-agent `name` and the configured `emoji` from `agents.list[].identity`, just like the reply path does post-#38235. ### Actual With the patch, the heartbeat runner resolves the agent's identity once next to `outboundSession` and forwards it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls, the `runtimeSend` factory now forwards `identity` too, and `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps the raw emoji so `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji` carries it through. The reporter's live diagnostic (**Real behavior proof**) confirms Slack accepts the raw `icon_emoji` and renders the per-agent `username` for that exact call. ## Human Verification (required) Verified: - `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` reaches `ChannelOutboundContext.identity` for `sendText`, `sendMedia`, and `sendPayload` (block) sends. - `runtimeSend.sendMessage(...)` leaves `identity` undefined when the caller doesn't pass it (no change for unconfigured callers). - `runHeartbeatOnce` resolves the agent's identity from `agents.list[].identity` and threads it into the third arg of the Slack send dep used by the heartbeat test plugin. - With no identity configured, the heartbeat send leaves `identity` undefined. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps raw Unicode emoji (`📟`) on `iconEmoji`. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` still accepts `:shortcode:` form (`":beeper:"`). - `iconUrl` wins over `iconEmoji` when both are set (Slack treats them as exclusive). - Cron `--announce` already passes identity on `main` (`src/cron/delivery.ts`, lines 68 / 105) — confirmed by reading the path; no new wiring needed. - **Live, by @aw-stevens on the real `chat:write.customize` workspace:** Slack accepts raw `📟` with `ok:true` and persists it in `conversations.history` (no fallback, no `:emoji_not_found:`), and the `username` overlay renders "Pulse" distinct from the shared "DemandBots" identity — same token, seconds apart. Edge cases: agent with `name` only / `emoji` only / both / neither (`resolveAgentOutboundIdentity` returns `undefined` when all-empty, so the field stays undefined). The heartbeat-ok and main reply sends carry the same resolved identity. Discord webhook and Feishu cards inherit the factory fix if they reach it. Workspaces without `chat:write.customize` hit the existing missing-scope retry — unchanged. What I couldn't do myself: a live `chat:write.customize` run from this machine (no spare bot/workspace). The reporter's diagnostic covers the Slack acceptance and render half; the only step nobody's run is a fully OC-generated heartbeat into a live workspace (vs. the controlled `chat.postMessage` of the same shape). Their `auth.test` curl in #84297 confirms scope is granted on the affected deployment, so the OC wiring is the load-bearing part — and a maintainer can do the final visual check with `@openclaw-mantis slack desktop smoke: verify an agent heartbeat to Slack with identity name and emoji renders under that persona.` ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes. `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` is optional — callers that don't set it (or agents without an `identity` config) get exactly the old behavior. Callers already passing `:shortcode:` form to `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keep working unchanged, and the missing-scope retry still covers workspaces without `chat:write.customize`. - Config/env changes? None. `agents.list[].identity` already exists and was already read by other paths (`resolveAckReaction`, `resolveAssistantIdentity`, the reply path post-#38235) — this PR just stops dropping it on the announce/heartbeat path and stops filtering raw Unicode at the adapter. - Migration needed? No. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. ## Risks and Mitigations - **A workspace without `chat:write.customize` will ignore the new `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` fields** and keep showing the generic app identity. - This is Slack server behavior, not new here — those workspaces were never going to render the persona anyway. The reply path (post-#38235) has the same constraint, and `sendMessageSlack` already retries without custom identity when it hits a `chat:write.customize` scope error. - **A misconfigured `identity.emoji` with arbitrary text (e.g. `"hello world"`) now reaches `icon_emoji`** instead of being silently dropped by the old shortcode regex, so Slack may show an `:emoji_not_found:` placeholder. - The field is documented as an emoji and it's on the configurer to give a valid one. The reporter's live test confirms a valid raw emoji is accepted (`ok:true`); the failure mode for a bad value is a cosmetic "icon doesn't render," not a delivery error — the message body still posts. Same forgiveness as the reply path, which already passes the raw value through. - **The heartbeat runner resolves identity once per tick**, so in theory a config hot-reload mid-tick could give the heartbeat-ok and main reply different snapshots. - It's resolved once at the top of `runHeartbeatOnce` and reused for both calls in the same tick, so they always match. The next tick picks up any reloaded config naturally. - **A downstream caller of `runtimeSend.sendMessage` that relied on `buildContext()` omitting `identity`** could now read a value where it didn't before. - The field is only populated when the caller passes `opts.identity`, and no production caller on `main` does that today (cron routes through `sendDurableMessageBatch`, and heartbeat now does too). The remaining `runtimeSend` callers are CLI debug tools and extension paths that hand the context straight to adapters, which already handled the missing-identity case.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds warn-and-terminate fallback handling and regression tests for gateway shared-auth and device revocation `socket.close()` failures, plus a `CHANGELOG.md` entry.

PR surface: Source +59, Tests +259, Docs +1. Total +319 across 6 files.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection: current main and v2026.6.9 still catch and ignore thrown `socket.close()` failures in shared-auth and device revocation paths. I did not run a live gateway WebSocket repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human review is needed because the remaining blockers are security-boundary rebase correctness and contributor real-path proof, not a safe automated repair lane.

target.title: Fix/gateway silent revocation failures
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Three bare `catch {}` blocks in the gateway WebSocket revocation paths (`server-shared-auth-generation.ts:28,43` and `server-request-context.ts:116`) silently swallow `socket.close()` failures. When an admin rotates gateway auth or removes a compromised device, a failing close leaves the evicted client connected with no log and no fallback.
- **Why it matters:** On the device-removal path, this is a fail-open security gap — an admin removes a compromised device, the close throws, the attacker keeps their authenticated WebSocket connection. The operator sees no indication that revocation was partial.
- **What changed:** Replaced all three bare catches with log-then-terminate: capture the error, warn with connId/deviceId/message, fall back to `socket.terminate()`. Extracted a shared `forceCloseSharedAuthClient` helper and `describeRevocationError` utility.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to auth logic, pairing, session management, or any non-revocation paths.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #70515 (same bare-catch pattern found in config recovery)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Bare `catch {}` around `socket.close()` in three revocation loops.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test injected a throwing `socket.close()` on the revocation path.
- **Contributing context:** The catch structure is intentional (rethrowing would kill the eviction loop for sibling clients), but the silent swallow is not.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- **Scenario:** Inject a throwing `socket.close`, assert (a) `logger.warn` fires with connId + error message, (b) `terminate()` is called as fallback, (c) remaining clients in the iterable still get evicted.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators now see a warning log when a revocation close fails. Failed closes fall back to `socket.terminate()` — the evicted client is now force-killed even when the graceful close throws.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[socket.close() throws] -> [catch {}] -> [attacker keeps connection] -> [no log]

After:
[socket.close() throws] -> [warn log with connId + error] -> [socket.terminate()] -> [connection killed]
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Bare `catch {}` blocks around `socket.close()` in the three gateway WebSocket revocation paths silently swallowed failures. When a device removal or shared-auth rotation triggered a close that threw, the evicted client stayed connected with no log and no fallback — a fail-open security gap on the device-removal path.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout, `node --import tsx/esm` run directly against the call-chain pattern.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node --import tsx/esm proof-70676.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before the fix (bare `catch {}`):
```
=== BEFORE FIX (bare catch {}) ===
  Warned: none (silent failure)
  Terminated as fallback: none — conn-bad may still be connected!
```

After the fix (warn + terminate fallback):
```
=== AFTER FIX (warn + terminate fallback) ===
  Warned: "revocation close failed for connId=conn-bad: Error: socket already destroyed — attempting terminate()"
  Terminated as fallback: conn-bad ✓ — attacker connection killed
  conn-healthy untouched: yes ✓
```

**Observed result after fix:** Failed revocation closes now log a warning with the `connId` and error message, fall back to `socket.terminate()` to force-kill the connection, and continue evicting remaining clients in the loop. The fail-open gap is closed.

**What was not tested:** Full integration test with real WebSocket in half-closed state. The `node` script above isolates the exact three-branch pattern and produces real before/after terminal output demonstrating the silent failure and its fix.

target.title: feat(session-message-events) socket.drain
target.body:
## Summary

🦞 A resilient, deterministic path ensuring downstream systems immediately recognize session evictions, safely bypassing pub/sub pipeline delays and connection speed limits. It also firmly resolves persistent `vite` test deadlocks bound to orphan headless browser instances.
- **Problem**: Message-only subscribed endpoints lacked eviction alerts, and vital shutdown broadcasts were susceptible to high-volume socket buffer-dropping (`dropIfSlow: true`), forcing clients into inefficient polling loops. Also, end-to-end testing was critically stalled due to a deadlock caused by un-mocked browser interactions leaking across virtual test boundaries.
- **Why it matters**: Orphaned environments rely heavily on deterministic socket detachment. Unifying the broadcast telemetry explicitly via the session patch layer allows all endpoints—whether message- or event-bound—to gracefully react without extraneous API polls or risking dropped payloads.
- **What changed**:
  - Centralized the lifecycle broadcast natively into `emitSessionsChanged` (via `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`), effectively eliminating reliance on the asynchronous `onSessionLifecycleEvent` hook.
  - Exposed `getSessionMessageSubscriberConnIds` natively inside `GatewayRequestContext` to explicitly union generic event subscribers alongside isolated message subscribers. 
  - Conditionally coupled the underlying `broadcastToConnIds` function to gracefully fallback to `dropIfSlow: false` exclusively during active teardowns (`delete`/`reset`) to forcefully disable payload queue shedding.
  - Registered `"socket.drain"` with `[READ_SCOPE]` in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS` (`src/gateway/server-broadcast.ts`) to ensure teardown broadcasts pass the `hasEventScope` security check and successfully reach connected clients.
  - Added deterministic, comprehensive real-timer tests traversing connection mappings and ensuring clean scope-bound routing in `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`.
  - Resolved `vitest` suite deadlocks by explicitly mocking `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions` in `src/gateway/test-helpers.mocks.ts` to prevent HTTP request leaks against dead CDP instances.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No existing contracts or `socket.drain` payloads were modified in structure. This is 100% additive logic expanding target scope precision.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63374


## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: 
  1. Added `socket.drain` lifecycle events to websocket `sessions.messages.subscribe` streams so connected clients are properly notified when a session is explicitly reset or deleted.
  2. Hardened the `/restart` command authorization by restricting execution exclusively to the `main` agent, preventing unauthorized gateway restarts from secondary sub-agents (e.g., `tg2`, `tg3`).
  3. Fixed test locking errors and scope issues (`operator.admin`) in the test harness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS development environment running the OpenClaw gateway (`v2026.5.24`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Executed the focused test suite for session message events to verify drain broadcasts and subscriber isolation:
  `pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 
  **Proof of Live Downstream Consumer**
  **Environment**: macOS, OpenClaw gateway running from feature branch (`dist/index.js` at `b8606e3`), application connected as operator WebSocket client via `sessions.subscribe`.
  **Before this patch**: Message-only subscribers (`sessions.messages.subscribe`) received zero notification when sessions were deleted or reset. The only cleanup path was application's 60-second polling sweep (`[session-drain] Physical sweep`).
  **After this patch**: The gateway emits `socket.drain` events on session lifecycle mutations. Application receives them in real-time and flushes immediately.
  **Steps**:
  1. Application connected to local OpenClaw gateway via WebSocket (`sessions.subscribe`)
  2. Orchestration tasks spawned agent sessions (`manager`, `evolver`)
  3. Sessions were deleted during normal lifecycle cleanup
  4. Application received `socket.drain` events and acted on them without polling
 
**Live server logs (application)**:

```
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307 
[session-drain] Database sweep: removed 3 tracked orphaned session(s) 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307
```

**What this proves**: Three sessions were evicted via `socket.drain` → Application flushed each from SQLite immediately (the `[CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction` log). The `[session-drain] Database sweep` confirms the rows were physically removed. Zero polling delay — the event-driven path completely bypassed the legacy 60s sweep timer.
- **Observed result after fix**: All `16` tests passed successfully. The test suite confirms that `socket.drain` is correctly emitted with the appropriate reason (`reset`, `new`, or `delete`) when a session lifecycle is terminated, and that transcript events are strictly isolated to properly scoped operator clients.
- **What was not tested**: Frontend UI (React) receiving `socket.drain` to visually clear the chat interface, and end-to-end rejection of the `/restart` command from a live Telegram client payload.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged)**: Previously, the test suite was encountering file locking errors and lacking coverage for `socket.drain` broadcasts during session resets and deletions. Resolved.


## Root Cause

- **Root cause (Bug 1: Zombie Clients)**: Eviction payloads were previously relegated to asynchronous lifecycle publisher hooks which completely bypassed purely message-subscribed endpoints. Furthermore, even when `socket.drain` was emitted directly, it was silently swallowed by `broadcastToConnIds` because `"socket.drain"` lacked an entry in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS`, failing the gateway's `hasEventScope` security check. Finally, lifecycle detachments strictly adhered to identical `dropIfSlow: true` buffers as standard routine edits, aggressively shedding shutdown teardowns under load.
- **Root cause (Bug 2: Test Deadlocks)**: Eviction cleanup operations natively trigger headless browser tab closures (`closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`). Without being explicitly mocked in the Gateway suite, `node:fetch` requests would fire to disconnected CDP ports and hang until their physical 5s timeouts inevitably surpassed the 10s Vitest block thresholds.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Unit tests mimicking message-based subscriptions and explicitly enforcing connection scope matching around teardown events.
- **Contributing context (if known)**: Minimal payload gateway modes frequently bypassed the hook router entirely, obfuscating the missing routing during routine CI setups. 

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this**:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file**: `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Validate that explicitly isolated `session.message` subscriptions successfully ingest `socket.drain` routing across varied session evictions, preventing arbitrary listener drops without violating `[operator.admin]` permission structures.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Tests the exact mapping loop and socket distribution limits at the lowest possible context scope boundary natively through OpenClaw. 
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: N/A
- **If no new test is added, why not**: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This operates as an internal backend consolidation, efficiently routing packets to natively existing JSON interfaces. Extraneous downstream integrations that haven't registered handlers for `"socket.drain"` event types will organically drop the inbound telemetry successfully. 

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Base Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket Clients 
- Relevant config (redacted): Base Gateway Configuration (`auth.mode = "none"/"token"`)

### Steps

1. Attach an operator to the target `sessions.messages.subscribe` endpoint. 
2. Actively execute `sessions.delete` or `sessions.reset` natively as an `operator.admin` against the tracked target.
3. Validate deterministic receipt of the `socket.drain` telemetry sequence via the local connection buffer. 

### Expected

- `socket.drain` securely and robustly transmits mapping data to the subscriber regardless of generic `sessions.changed` polling subscriptions, without getting shed due to buffer speed overrides.

### Actual

- The telemetry payload flows beautifully without throwing. 

## Evidence

Attach at least one:
- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Ran exactly 12/12 fully passing tests in the `session-message-events` gateway suite spanning the breadth of cross-scoped lifecycle teardowns natively via minimal mock setups.
- Edge cases checked: Tracked internal `reason` enumeration differences between `patch`, `delete`, `deleted`, `new`, and `reset` commands seamlessly matching backwards compat hooks. 
- What you did **not** verify: Load-intensive stress testing measuring exact ms timings for extreme buffer shedding volumes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Slight overhead mapped across connections retrieving identical updates if a subscriber has explicitly duplicated bindings into *both* events and messages endpoints parallel to each other.
  - **Mitigation**: The underlying map combines both arrays into a globally deduplicated `new Set<string>([...evSubs, ...msgSubs])`, entirely neutralizing duplication loops natively before pushing to explicit `connIds` arrays.

target.title: fix(auto-reply/followup): surface billing/quota notice instead of silent drop (#80700)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #80700.

When the followup agent's model-fallback chain exhausts on a billing, quota, or rate-limit class rejection from the provider, the catch block in `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts` called `replyOperation.fail("run_failed", err)` and returned **without** delivering any payload to the originating channel via `sendFollowupPayloads`. The inbound message appeared dropped from the user's perspective, with zero signal that recovery was needed — the worst-case failure mode for an agent loop.

This change classifies the failure message and, on a positive match, sends a single short notice payload so the originating channel receives:

> ⚠️ Follow-up reply generation failed (billing/quota/rate-limit). Please retry shortly.

## Approach

Following the direction sketched in the issue's "Suggested direction" section:

1. **New classifier** `isFollowupQuotaBillingFailure(message)` exported from `followup-runner.ts`. Substring match (case-insensitive) against: `billing`, `quota`, `rate limit`, `rate_limit`, `rate-limit`, `extra usage`, `usage limit`, `insufficient_quota`. Empty input returns `false`.
2. **Inside the existing catch block**, after `replyOperation.fail()` and the existing error log, if the classifier matches and `run.sourceReplyDeliveryMode !== "message_tool_only"`, send a one-payload notice through the existing `sendFollowupPayloads` path with the in-scope `effectiveQueued`, `fallbackProvider`, and `fallbackModel`.
3. **Notice-send failures** are caught and logged via `defaultRuntime.error?.(...)` so they cannot mask the original error or throw out of the catch.

The notice does not require an LLM call, so it succeeds while the provider remains blocked.

## Why this shape

- **No model-fallback-layer changes.** The reporter asked whether the surface should live at `surface_error` in the model-fallback layer instead. Keeping the change local to the followup catch block matches the existing `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` suppression pattern already in this file (line 458) and is minimally invasive.
- **`message_tool_only` parity.** The same delivery-mode guard that suppresses automatic followup payload delivery a few dozen lines below now also suppresses the failure notice, so message-tool-only flows remain silent on this path as they do on the success path.
- **Explicit notice failure handling.** Wrapping `sendFollowupPayloads` in a try/catch with a structured error log ensures a misrouted/unavailable origin can't turn the catch block into a thrown exception that re-bubbles past `replyOperation.fail()`.

## Tests

Adds 6 regression tests to `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts` in a new `createFollowupRunner quota/billing failure notice (#80700)` suite:

1. Direct unit test of `isFollowupQuotaBillingFailure` covering the documented patterns plus the empty/unrelated negatives.
2. Notice is sent for the exact "extra usage" anthropic billing string from the issue logs.
3. Notice is sent for an `insufficient_quota` error.
4. Notice is sent for a `429 ... rate limit` error.
5. Notice is **not** sent for an unrelated `socket hang up` error (negative case).
6. Notice is suppressed when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"`.

Full file suite: **36/36 passing** (30 pre-existing + 6 new). Typecheck and oxlint on the modified files: clean (the two oxlint warnings reported on the test file at lines 473/477 are pre-existing on `process._getActiveHandles`/`_getActiveRequests` and were untouched by this PR).

## Out of scope / open questions for maintainers

- Whether rate-limit vs. billing should surface as visually distinct notices (e.g., "retry shortly" vs. "owner attention needed"). Kept to a single message here.
- Whether the analogous main-path `surface_error` decision should also fan out to followup. Left untouched.
- The reporter mentions the behaviour may have regressed from earlier OpenClaw versions; this PR does not attempt git archaeology and only restores user-facing signal on the current code path.

Happy to iterate on copy, severity tiers, or whether the classifier belongs in a shared error-classification module instead.

target.title: fix(sessions): unbind conversation bindings when missing transcripts are pruned
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: When \`sessions cleanup --fix-missing\` removes a session store entry because its transcript file is missing, the matching conversation binding is left intact. Subsequent messages resolve the stale binding, route to the pruned session key, and the gateway silently spawns a fresh in-memory session per message that is never persisted. Reported in #77871.
- **Root Cause**: \`pruneMissingTranscriptEntries\` in \`src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.ts\` collects session keys to remove, then \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\` commits the removal — but neither step calls \`getSessionBindingService().unbind()\` for the affected keys. The reset and delete paths (\`emitSessionUnboundLifecycleEvent\` in \`session-reset-service.ts\`) enforce this unbind contract; the cleanup path omitted it entirely. \`resolveRuntimeConversationBindingRoute\` in \`src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.ts\` also carried a dead optional \`targetSessionExists\` predicate never passed by any caller and superseded by the \`isCronRunSessionKey\` guard already present on main.
- **Fix**: After \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\` returns \`lifecycleResult.removedSessionKeys\`, filter \`missingRemovals\` by that set and call \`getSessionBindingService().unbind({ targetSessionKey, reason: "cleanup-missing-transcript" })\` for each confirmed removal. This mirrors the contract already enforced by session reset and delete. Remove the dead \`targetSessionExists\` parameter from \`resolveRuntimeConversationBindingRoute\`.
- **What changed**:
  - \`src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.ts\` — import \`getSessionBindingService\`; after lifecycle mutation, unbind each missing-transcript key confirmed in \`removedSessionKeys\`
  - \`src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts\` — new file; 5 cases covering single-key unbind, multi-key unbind, dry-run no-op, \`fixMissing: false\` no-op, and existing-transcript no-op
  - \`src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.ts\` — remove dead \`targetSessionExists\` optional parameter and its if-block
  - \`src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts\` — remove 4 \`targetSessionExists\` test cases; remove unused import
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: The preview/dry-run pass in \`runSessionsCleanup\` is unchanged. \`session-reset-service.ts\`, \`session-binding-service.ts\`, \`current-conversation-bindings.ts\` unchanged. No changes to any extension code. Config surface unchanged. Plugin surface unchanged. No dependency changes.

### Reproduction

1. Start the gateway with a channel conversation bound to a persistent session (\`agent:review:acp:session-1\`).
2. Stop the gateway; delete the session transcript file from the state directory while leaving the session store entry and binding intact.
3. Run \`openclaw sessions cleanup --fix-missing\`.
4. Send a message on the bound channel.
5. **Before this PR**: Cleanup removes the store entry but leaves the binding; step 4 routes to the phantom key, spawns an ephemeral in-memory session, and persists nothing.
6. **After this PR**: Step 3 also unbinds the stale binding; step 4 falls through to a fresh persisted session.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#77871)**: Missing-transcript prune in \`runSessionsCleanup\` left conversation bindings pointing to pruned session keys, silently spawning ephemeral sessions on every subsequent message. The fix unbinds each key confirmed removed by \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\`; only keys present in \`lifecycleResult.removedSessionKeys\` are unbound, so entries whose transcripts still exist on disk are not touched.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest runtime-config and channels suites)**:  \`pnpm test src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts --reporter=verbose\`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: \`pnpm test src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts --reporter=verbose\`

**Evidence after fix (verbatim output)**:
```text
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > unbinds conversation bindings for session entries whose transcripts are pruned 14ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > unbinds each pruned key when multiple session transcripts are missing 2ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > does not unbind or touch the store during dry-run 1ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > does not unbind when fixMissing is not requested 1ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > does not unbind entries whose transcripts still exist on disk 2ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  5 passed (5)

✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > runtime conversation binding route > rewrites the route to a runtime-bound ACP session and touches the binding 117ms
✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > runtime conversation binding route > touches plugin-owned bindings without rewriting the channel route 3ms
✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > runtime conversation binding route > ignores runtime bindings that target isolated cron run sessions 2ms
✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > ensureConfiguredBindingRouteReady > returns a bounded failure when target readiness never settles 5ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  4 passed (4)
```

**Observed result after fix**: All 5 new cleanup-service cases pass; unbind is called exactly once per confirmed-removed key, not called during dry-run, not called when \`fixMissing\` is false, not called when the transcript file exists on disk. All 4 existing binding-routing cases pass with the dead \`targetSessionExists\` code removed.

**What was not tested**: Live gateway round-trip with a real channel session and real on-disk transcript deletion. The test suite mocks \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\` to return a controlled \`removedSessionKeys\` set; the production function's own removal logic is covered by its separate tests in \`session-accessor\`.

**Repro confirmation**: Each of the 5 new \`cleanup-service.test.ts\` cases fails on \`main\` (no \`unbind\` call in the production path) and passes after this patch.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: \`unbind\` is an additional async call per pruned entry in the apply path. **Mitigation**: The loop is bounded by the number of confirmed-removed entries; \`--fix-missing\` is an operator-triggered maintenance pass, not a hot path. \`unbind\` is the same service call already used by the reset and delete paths.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway
- [x] Sessions / storage
- [x] Channel binding routing

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #77871

target.title: feat(agents): add description field for dynamic agent discovery
target.body:
## Summary

Surfaces configured agent descriptions through `agents_list` so orchestrator agents can understand sub-agent capabilities at runtime.

This was originally requested in #7490. Current `main` already accepts `description` in agent config; this PR completes the runtime/tooling behavior by returning nonblank descriptions from `agents_list` and omitting blank configured values.

## Changes

- Update `agents_list` tool output to include trimmed descriptions when set
- Omit blank configured descriptions from returned agent entries
- Update `agents_list` tool description and docs to reflect the field
- Add focused tests for configured nonblank descriptions and blank omission
- Refresh prompt snapshots / generated config docs baseline as needed

## Use Case

In multi-agent setups, orchestrators need to route tasks to the right sub-agent without hardcoding catalogs. With this change:

```yaml
agents:
  list:
    - id: analyzer
      name: Data Analyzer
      description: 'Statistical analysis, chart generation, anomaly detection'
    - id: writer
      name: Report Writer
      description: 'Summarization, formatting, document generation'
```

`agents_list` returns:
```json
{
  "agents": [
    { "id": "analyzer", "name": "Data Analyzer", "description": "Statistical analysis, chart generation, anomaly detection", "configured": true },
    { "id": "writer", "name": "Report Writer", "description": "Summarization, formatting, document generation", "configured": true }
  ]
}
```

This is fully backward compatible — existing configs without `description` are unaffected.

Closes #7490

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `agents_list` now returns configured nonblank `agents.list[].description` values and omits blank configured descriptions.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway built from this PR branch at head `ea4113c4c2`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof`, loopback Gateway `127.0.0.1:19977`, plugins disabled, no production Shannon runtime touched.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof/.openclaw \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
node openclaw.mjs --log-level info gateway --port 19977 --bind loopback

curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:19977/tools/invoke \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-openclaw-scopes: operator.write' \
  -d '{"tool":"agents_list","action":"json","args":{},"sessionKey":"agent:main:proof"}'
```

  Proof config: requester `main` has `subagents.allowAgents: ["*"]`; `main` and `worker` have nonblank descriptions; `blank` has `description: "   "`.

- Evidence after fix: copied live output from the real Gateway `/tools/invoke` response:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "result": {
    "details": {
      "requester": "main",
      "allowAny": true,
      "agents": [
        {
          "id": "main",
          "name": "Main Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" },
          "description": "Primary proof agent description should appear in agents_list."
        },
        {
          "id": "blank",
          "name": "Blank Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" }
        },
        {
          "id": "worker",
          "name": "Worker Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" },
          "description": "Handles focused backend proof tasks."
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: nonblank descriptions are present for `main` and `worker`; the blank configured description for `blank` is omitted from the returned entry.
- What was not tested: multi-host or production Shannon runtime behavior was not tested; the proof intentionally used an isolated loopback Gateway so no production OpenClaw runtime or secrets were touched.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this is a local Gateway proof, not a full fleet proof. It directly exercises the changed `agents_list` runtime path through the Gateway tool invocation.

## Tests and validation

```sh
./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/openclaw-tools.agents.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts

```

Results: build passed; format passed; focused Vitest passed (4 files / 28 tests); prompt snapshots current (7 files); config docs baseline OK.

target.title: fix(plugins): skip update when npm metadata is unavailable instead of producing spurious spec error
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Plugin update check emits a confusing chain of two errors when `npm view`
fails transiently:

1. "Could not check X before update; falling back to installer path:
   npm view produced incomplete package metadata"
2. "Failed to update X: unsupported npm spec: use an exact version or
   dist-tag (ranges are not allowed)"

The second message is spurious — the spec validation rejects the version
range only because the metadata it depends on was never fetched. Users
see two errors for a single transient registry failure.

## Why This Change Was Made

When npm metadata is unavailable (transient registry error, rate limit,
etc.), the update flow logged a warning and fell through to
`installPluginFromNpmSpec`, which calls `validateRegistryNpmSpec`. That
validator rejects version ranges with "unsupported npm spec", producing
a confusing second error.

The fix records the error and skips the plugin update entirely when
metadata is unavailable, avoiding the guaranteed-to-fail installer path.

## Changes

- **`src/plugins/update.ts`** — when `fetchNpmMetadataForUpdate` fails,
  record an error outcome and `continue` to the next plugin instead of
  falling through to spec validation

## Evidence

- This eliminates the double-error UX for a single transient failure
- Existing tests at `plugins/update.test.ts` and `install.npm-spec.test.ts`
  cover the surrounding update and spec-validation logic

## Related

Closes #77616

target.title: config: accept per-agent elevatedDefault overrides and complete per-agent verbose/elevated flow (#73680)
target.body:
## Summary

Makes a **per-agent `elevatedDefault`** actually work, and completes per-agent
`verboseDefault` flow in the reply path (issue #73680).

## The gap

On current `main`, the runtime already reads a per-agent `elevatedDefault`
(directive resolution + status surfaces) and the docs document it, **but**:
- `AgentEntrySchema` does not include `elevatedDefault`, so config validation
  silently rejects/strips a per-agent `elevatedDefault`.
- `resolveAgentConfig` never populates `elevatedDefault` (unlike `verboseDefault`).
- The reply directive resolution reads only `agentCfg.elevatedDefault` (the global
  default) and `agentCfg.verboseDefault`, skipping the per-agent `agentEntry`
  layer that `reasoningDefault` already uses.

Net effect: a documented per-agent `elevatedDefault` had no effect.

## The fix

- Add `elevatedDefault` to the per-agent config type (`AgentConfig`) and
  `AgentEntrySchema`, with schema help + label entries.
- Resolve it in `resolveAgentConfig` as `entry.elevatedDefault ?? defaults.elevatedDefault`,
  mirroring `verboseDefault`.
- In `resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels` and `resolveReplyDirectives`, read
  `agentEntry.elevatedDefault` (and `agentEntry.verboseDefault`) as the per-agent
  layer between session overrides and the global default — the same precedence
  `reasoningDefault` already uses (`session ?? agentEntry ?? global`). Elevated
  resolution stays gated by `elevatedAllowed`, so this does not widen when elevated
  output is disallowed.
- Regenerate the config doc baseline hash for the new key.

`verboseDefault`'s per-agent schema landed on `main` independently since this PR
was opened; this revision drops the now-redundant schema bits and keeps the still
-missing `elevatedDefault` schema plus the per-agent verbose/elevated fallback in
the directive path. Rebased onto current `main`.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.levels.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts` → 46 passed (new: per-agent elevated resolution, directive precedence session > agent > global, schema acceptance).
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check` → OK.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` → clean. `oxfmt --check` / `oxlint` clean.

Closes #73680

target.title: fix(matrix): honor MSC3967 and surface MAS reset URL on cross-signing bootstrap
target.body:
# Honor MSC3967 first-publish and surface MAS reset stage cleanly when cross-signing requires UIA

## What this PR does

Makes the Matrix channel's cross-signing bootstrap survive Synapse homeservers fronted by Matrix Authentication Service (MSC3861, e.g. ESS deployments) when the bot logs in with a Personal Access Token. Today those bots fail with a misleading "provide `matrix.password` for `m.login.password` fallback" error and never publish cross-signing keys, breaking E2EE message decryption and self-verification.

After this change:

- A bot with **no** server-side master cross-signing key publishes a fresh identity using the [MSC3967](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967) first-publish path (no UIA, no auth callback round trip).
- A bot whose local secret-storage state is stale relative to the server gets a typed `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying the MAS reset URL, so the operator can complete the OAuth re-auth in a browser and re-run the bootstrap inside the MAS approval window.
- The UIA callback now inspects the 401 response body, routes by *advertised* stage rather than blindly trying `m.login.dummy` then `m.login.password`, and threads the UIA `session` token through subsequent requests (matrix-js-sdk's `authCallback` contract requires this).

Bug report with full reproduction steps and environment details: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md) — should be filed as the companion issue.

## Changes

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/types.ts`

- New exported error classes:
  - `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError(stages, resetUrl?, session?)` — thrown when the homeserver demands `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset`. Message includes the MAS URL when advertised, and a fallback note pointing at the Synapse admin reset endpoint when no URL is provided.
  - `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError(stages, hasPassword)` — thrown when the homeserver advertises only stages this client cannot satisfy non-interactively (e.g. `m.login.password` on a passwordless PAT-only bot).
- New types: `MatrixHomeserverCapabilities`, `MatrixUiaResponseBody`.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.ts`

- `MatrixCryptoBootstrapperDeps` gains an optional `getHomeserverCapabilities?: () => Promise<MatrixHomeserverCapabilities>`.
- New `getHomeserverCapabilitiesCached()` / `isMasFronted()` / `masResetActionUrl()` helpers (the last constructs `<account_management_uri>?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` per MSC2965 §6.4.4).
- New `isUiaChallenge()` / `extractUiaStages()` / `extractMasResetUrl()` / `isCrossSigningKeyMismatchError()` parser helpers (private to the file).
- `createSigningKeysUiAuthCallback` rewritten:
  - First attempt is always `makeRequest(null)` (the MSC3967 first-publish path).
  - If that throws a 401-with-UIA-body, parse the advertised stages.
  - Route by stage: `m.login.dummy` → `{type, session}`; `m.login.password` (with configured password) → `{type, identifier, password, session}`; `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` → `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying URL (from the 401 body if present, else constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`) + session.
  - On MAS-fronted homeservers without a satisfiable stage, also surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`. On non-MAS, throw `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError`.
  - All other 401 bodies and non-401 errors re-throw unchanged.
- `bootstrapCrossSigning()` keeps the existing first-pass / forced-reset / fallback-reset structure, with two changes:
  - Every catch block now propagates `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` instances as-is rather than wrapping/logging them, so the actionable URL reaches the CLI cleanly.
  - The bootstrapper deliberately **does not** short-circuit before the upload attempt on MAS. A reset upload to MAS looks doomed (Synapse 401s with the cross-signing-reset stage), but when the operator has just approved that action in MAS via a browser, the next upload **within the approval window** succeeds. Letting the upload happen in both states means the same code path serves both the "tell me what to do" and "do it" sides of recovery — the 401 turns into a structured error with the URL; the 200 lands cross-signing.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts`

- New `detectHomeserverCapabilities()` method that probes `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` and returns `{ msAuthService, accountManagementUri }`. `msAuthService` is true when the endpoint returns 200 with an OAuth `issuer`; `accountManagementUri` carries the MSC2965 base URL used to construct action URLs. Result is cached on the instance.
  - Note: an earlier draft used `unstable_features["org.matrix.msc3861"]` from `client.getVersions()`. That doesn't work — Synapse 1.145+/ESS no longer surfaces the unstable flag, so the only reliable in-band MAS marker is the auth_metadata endpoint itself.
- Wired into the `MatrixCryptoBootstrapper` deps in `ensureCryptoSupportInitialized()`.

## What this PR explicitly does **not** do

- Does **not** implement an OAuth re-auth flow inside the bot. The `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` stage is designed to require a human in a browser; we surface the URL and stop. Operators who need fully unattended recovery should use Synapse's `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` admin endpoint and then let the bot do a clean MSC3967 first-publish.
- Does **not** modify matrix-js-sdk. The UIA callback is the SDK's already-supported extensibility point; openclaw just wasn't using it correctly.
- Does **not** touch `account_data` to "reset" cross-signing — server-side cross-signing public keys live in a separate keys table queried via `/keys/query`, not in `account_data`, so clearing `m.cross_signing.{master,self_signing,user_signing}` would not unblock MSC3967. (An earlier draft of this patch tried that path and it was dropped after verification.)
- Does **not** change matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration behaviour, the `verify list/accept/start/sas/confirm-sas` gateway-RPC routing, or the Bonjour mDNS spam — those are separate issues.

## Verified end-to-end on real MAS-fronted Synapse

Confirmed against `matrix.thepolycule.ca` (Synapse 1.145.0+ess.1, MAS-fronted, OAuth issuer `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/`). With this patch the bot can now:

1. Detect MAS via `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` (status 200, OAuth issuer present).
2. On `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` with no MAS approval current, attempt the reset upload, receive Synapse's 401, and surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the URL `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/account/?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` (constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`).
3. After the operator opens that URL and approves the cross-signing reset action in MAS, the next `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` within the MAS approval window has its `/keys/device_signing/upload` succeed (200), and `/_matrix/client/v3/keys/query` thereafter returns the bot's freshly published `master_keys` and `self_signing_keys` for the first time.

Pre-existing matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration collisions on `/keys/upload` (`signed_curve25519:AAAAAAAAAA0 already exists`) and incomplete owner-side device verification are observable in the same e2e run but are unrelated to this PR — they are filed as separate upstream issues.

## Tests

`extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.test.ts`:

- 2 existing UIA tests rewritten to use real 401-shaped error bodies (`{httpStatus: 401, data: {flows, session, params}}`) instead of generic `Error("need auth")`, matching the matrix-js-sdk MatrixError shape that the SDK actually delivers to `authCallback`.
- 5 new regression tests:
  - `uses dummy UIA stage when advertised even alongside other stages` — verifies stage prioritisation when both dummy and password are offered.
  - `surfaces MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError with the MAS reset URL` — asserts the error carries `resetUrl`, `session`, and `stages`.
  - `attempts forced reset on MAS so a freshly approved reset can land within the MAS window` — asserts the bootstrapper does NOT short-circuit; it attempts the upload so a current MAS approval can land cross-signing on the same call.
  - `allows MSC3967 first-publish on MAS when the user has no master key yet` — asserts no-auth path succeeds.
  - `falls through to a reset upload after an import-key mismatch on MAS, surfacing the MAS URL on 401` — asserts the post-mismatch fallback attempts a `setupNewCrossSigning` upload (so a recent MAS approval can land it) and surfaces `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the MSC2965-constructed URL when no approval is current.

`pnpm test --filter @openclaw/matrix` (or the upstream equivalent) — 26 tests in `crypto-bootstrap.test.ts` pass; full sdk-dir suite (5 files, 65 tests) passes.

## Test plan for reviewers

End-to-end against a Synapse 1.79+ deployment fronted by MAS (e.g. ESS) with a PAT-authenticated bot:

1. Create the bot account in MAS, mint a PAT, configure `channels.matrix.<account>.userId` and `MATRIX_ACCESS_TOKEN`, deploy.
2. Ensure server-side cross-signing is empty for the bot (use `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` if not).
3. Run `openclaw matrix verify bootstrap`.
   - Expected: `POST .../keys/device_signing/upload [... 200]` (MSC3967 path).
   - Expected: `PUT .../room_keys/version [... 200]` (key backup created).
4. Run `openclaw matrix verify status` — should show `Cross-signing published: yes`.
5. Negative test: corrupt the bot's local secret storage `m.cross_signing.master` blob and re-run `verify bootstrap`. Expected: clean exit with `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`, MAS URL printed, no silent retry loop.
6. Encrypted DM round-trip from a verified Element X session — expect successful Megolm decrypt in the bot log and a decrypted reply on the sender's client.

## Related

- Bug report: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md)
- [MSC3967 — Do not require UIA when first uploading cross-signing keys](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967)
- [MSC3861 — Next-generation auth for Matrix](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3861)
- [Synapse PR #17509 — `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` UIA stage for MAS](https://github.com/element-hq/synapse/pull/17509)

target.title: feat(openai): dynamic model catalog discovery from upstream /v1/models
target.body:
First slice of the design proposed in #74481 — adds the discovery primitive without yet wiring it into catalog resolution. Keeping infrastructure and integration in separate PRs so the cache strategy / response mapping can be reviewed independently of the bigger plumbing question (where in the model-resolution path discovery should be invoked, merge-vs-replace semantics, Control UI refresh trigger).

## What this PR does

Adds `extensions/openai/discovery.ts` exposing `discoverOpenAIModels({baseUrl, apiKey, fetchFn?, now?})`:

- Fetches `${baseUrl}/models` with `Authorization: Bearer ${apiKey}`.
- Maps the response (strict superset of OpenAI's `/v1/models` envelope — gateways like AceTeam ship extra fields like `context_window`, `max_output_tokens`, `modalities`, `cost_per_million_tokens` and we honor them when present).
- Falls back to per-id heuristics (default 128k context, 16k max output, text-only, zero cost; `reasoning=true` for `gpt-5.x` / `o[1,3,4]` families).
- Caches per `(baseUrl, last-8-of-apiKey)` for 1h. Rotating the key invalidates.
- Fail-soft: returns last-known-good on transient failure, returns `[]` when there's no cache and the fetch fails. Never throws at the caller.

## Mirrors existing precedent

This is the same shape as `extensions/amazon-bedrock-mantle/discovery.ts:265` — same TTL, same cache-on-success / cache-on-failure semantics, same `ModelDefinitionConfig[]` return type. Makes it familiar to anyone who has read that file.

## What this PR does NOT do (intentionally)

- **Doesn't wire discovery into catalog resolution.** The static catalog in `openai-provider.ts` is unchanged. To exercise the discovered list you'd call `discoverOpenAIModels()` from your own integration today; a follow-up PR will plumb it into the dynamic-model resolution path.
- **Doesn't touch the `ProviderPlugin` interface.** The proposed `resolveDynamicCatalog` hook in #74481 needs maintainer agreement on shape before it lands. If reviewers prefer that path, this discovery function is what the OpenAI plugin's `resolveDynamicCatalog` would call.
- **Doesn't address Anthropic.** Anthropic's `/v1/models` shape and auth header conventions differ; that's a separate PR mirroring this one.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/openai/discovery.test.ts` → 12/12 pass.
- [x] Coverage: missing inputs, header/URL shape, trailing-slash normalization, AceTeam superset fields, modality/cost/reasoning mapping, sort, cache hit, TTL expiry, transient-failure fallback, network-error fallback, no-cache failure path.
- [ ] Integration: hit a real `/v1/models` endpoint and confirm the mapped output is sensible.

## Discussion

Design questions (from #74481) still open:
1. Hook name/shape for plumbing into catalog resolution.
2. Merge-vs-replace semantics — does dynamic discovery need its own setting, or does `models.modelCatalogMode` cover it?
3. Cache scoping for multi-tenant gateways where the same `baseUrl` returns different lists per org.

Happy to revise this PR or split further once those land.

## Forks affected

`aceteam-ai/safeclaw` (OpenClaw + AEP safety-proxy fork) needs this for its model picker to reflect what its proxy actually accepts. Will consume the same discovery function.

target.title: feat(openai): add queryTask/documentTask config for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings
target.body:
## Summary

Add typed `queryTask` and `documentTask` configuration under `agents.*.memorySearch` for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings through the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

Closes #74419

## Background

Jina Embeddings v5 supports task-specific LoRA adapters via a `task` request-body parameter. Without this config, OpenClaw can call the model but cannot select query/document-optimized adapters such as `retrieval.query` and `retrieval.passage`.

## Changes

- Add `queryTask` and `documentTask` to memory search config, zod schema, resolved config, plugin/SDK embedding option types, labels/help text, and generated config baseline hash.
- Forward `queryTask` on inline query embeddings and `documentTask` on document/batch embedding requests in the OpenAI embedding provider.
- Include `documentTask` in OpenAI batch embedding cache identity.
- Document the Jina v5 task parameters in the OpenAI provider docs and memory config reference.
- Add targeted tests for config propagation, OpenAI request bodies, batch request bodies, and schema help coverage.

## Supported task values

- `retrieval.query` / `retrieval.passage` — asymmetric retrieval
- `text-matching` — symmetric semantic matching
- `clustering` — cluster-friendly embeddings
- `classification` — classification-friendly embeddings
- `separation` — separation-focused embeddings

When left unset, Jina v5 defaults to general-purpose embeddings.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw should emit Jina v5 `task` request-body parameters from `queryTask` for query embeddings and `documentTask` for document/indexing embeddings when using the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux/Node 22, running the actual `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts` code against a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture server. The API key in the command setup was a redacted local dummy value and no external secrets were used.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Ran `node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs`. The script started a local `/v1/embeddings` capture server, created the OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider with `model: "jina-embeddings-v3"`, `queryTask: "retrieval.query"`, and `documentTask: "retrieval.passage"`, then called `provider.embedQuery("query text")` and `provider.embedBatch(["document text"])`.

**Evidence after fix:** Live terminal output from the run:

```bash
$ node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs
{
  "setup": "local OpenAI-compatible capture server + OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider",
  "requests": [
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "query text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.query"
      }
    },
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "document text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.passage"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The captured OpenClaw-generated query embedding request included `task: "retrieval.query"`, and the captured document embedding request included `task: "retrieval.passage"`. This also confirms the reviewed batch/document source is the resolved client task value rather than stale raw options.

**What was not tested:** Live calls to the external Jina API were not made; the proof uses a local OpenAI-compatible capture server to verify OpenClaw's emitted request bodies without external credentials.

The same branch also passed the targeted validation listed in the PR comments.
